MW Tunnel] P1 -->|Greater than 10 cm| P4[Bulk Powder or
Loose Granules] P4 --> P5[Use 27.12 MHz
RF Capacitive] P2 --> P6[Reheating
Ready Meals] P3 --> P7[Pasteurisation
Bacon Patties] P5 --> P8[Post-Bake
Drying Crackers] style P0 fill:#e1f5ff style P8 fill:#c8e6c9 ``` # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Differentiate between microwave and radio frequency heating with respect to frequency range, penetration depth and electrode requirement.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Feature | Microwave | Radio Frequency | |---|---|---| | Frequency | 896/915 MHz, 2450 MHz | 13.56 / 27.12 / 40.68 MHz | | Penetration depth | 1 – 10 cm | 20 – 100+ cm | | Energy delivery | Radiative (waveguide + cavity) | Capacitive (between two electrodes) | | Heating volume | Small-to-medium | Very large bulk solids | > The lower the frequency, the deeper the wave penetrates; RF uses capacitive plates to inject the field, whereas MW uses a resonant cavity or applicator. ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]** **Define loss tangent. Why is it important in microwave heating of foods?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **loss tangent** is defined as $$ \tan \delta \;=\; \frac{\varepsilon''}{\varepsilon'} $$ where ε′ is the dielectric constant and ε″ the loss factor. It represents the **ratio of energy dissipated as heat to energy stored** in the dielectric per cycle. A higher tan δ means more efficient conversion of electromagnetic energy into thermal energy. Foods with high moisture (water has tan δ ≈ 0.12 at 2.45 GHz) heat rapidly, whereas dry or frozen foods heat very slowly — a fact exploited in *selective* tempering of frozen foods. ## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified]** **(a)** Derive the expression for volumetric power density generated in a food material exposed to a high-frequency electric field. State clearly the meaning of each term. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A 2450 MHz microwave oven delivers 800 W to 200 g of a food having c_p = 3500 J/kg·K. If 65 % of the supplied energy is absorbed by the food, compute the temperature rise after 60 seconds. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (a) Starting from the time-averaged energy dissipation in a lossy dielectric: $$ P_v \;=\; \omega \, \varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon'' \, E_{rms}^{2} $$ Substitute $\omega = 2\pi f$: $$ P_v \;=\; 2\pi f \, \varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon'' \, E_{rms}^{2} \;\; \text{W/m}^3 $$ Where: - $P_v$ = volumetric power density (W/m³) - $f$ = frequency of the alternating field (Hz) - $\varepsilon_0$ = permittivity of free space = 8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m - $\varepsilon''$ = relative dielectric loss factor of the food - $E_{rms}$ = root-mean-square electric field strength (V/m) In engineering units this is written as: $$ P_v \;=\; 55.61 \times 10^{-12}\, f\, \varepsilon''\, E_{rms}^{2} $$ A higher frequency, loss factor, or field intensity linearly/quadratically increases the power density — explaining why high-moisture foods cook fastest at 2.45 GHz. **(7 marks fully explained)** #### Model Solution (b) **Step 1 — Energy delivered to food in 60 s** $$ E_{delivered} \;=\; P \times t \;=\; 800 \times 60 \;=\; 48{,}000 \;\; \text{J} $$ **Step 2 — Energy absorbed (65 %)** $$ E_{abs} \;=\; 0.65 \times 48{,}000 \;=\; 31{,}200 \;\; \text{J} $$ **Step 3 — Temperature rise** $$ \Delta T \;=\; \frac{E_{abs}}{m \cdot c_p} \;=\; \frac{31{,}200}{0.2 \times 3500} $$ $$ \Delta T \;=\; \frac{31{,}200}{700} \;\approx\; 44.6 \;^{\circ}\text{C} $$ Hence the food temperature rises by **≈ 44.6 °C** in one minute. **(7 marks)** --- ### Question B (Alternative, 14 marks) **[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]** **(a)** Explain the mechanism of heat generation in foods during microwave heating. Discuss the role of dipolar rotation and ionic conduction with a suitable diagram. **(7 marks)** **(b)** List and briefly explain any **six industrial applications** of microwave and radio frequency processing in the food industry. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (a) When food is exposed to a high-frequency alternating electric field, two principal phenomena occur simultaneously: 1. **Dipolar Rotation (Polarisation Losses):** Polar molecules such as water possess a permanent electric dipole moment. Under an alternating field they try to follow the field reversal. Because of viscous drag and intermolecular forces, they cannot keep pace, and the resulting phase lag produces frictional energy dissipation quantified by the loss factor ε″. The energy dissipated per cycle per unit volume is $P_v = 2\pi f \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon'' E^{2}$. 2. **Ionic Conduction:** Dissolved salts dissociate into Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻, etc. These ions migrate in the alternating field, collide with neighbouring molecules, and convert kinetic energy into heat. The effect grows with ionic strength and is dominant in brine or salted foods. The combined effect produces **volumetric (in-situ) heating** with the heat generated throughout the bulk rather than transferred from the surface. > A clear block diagram (cavity → field → dipole + ion → heat) is expected — *see Section 4.1*. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution (b) — Six Industrial Applications 1. **Tempering of frozen foods** – 915 MHz selectively heats the water film on ice crystals without runaway thawing. 2. **Pasteurisation of ready-meals and sausages** – in-package pasteurisation extends shelf-life while preserving sensory quality. 3. **Pre-cooking of bacon and meat patties** – controlled volumetric heating reduces fry-time and yield loss. 4. **Drying of pasta, crackers and snack foods** – RF post-bake drying removes interior moisture without scorching the surface. 5. **Baking of bread, cookies and biscuits** – 915 MHz tunnels shorten bake time and improve crumb structure. 6. **Continuous in-flow pasteurisation of milk and juices** – tubular MW heaters at 915 MHz. 7. **(Bonus) Blanching of vegetables** – volumetric heating inactivates enzymes with minimal leaching loss. **(Any six = 7 marks; extra credit for the seventh.)** ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark Deductions (Read Carefully):** > 1. **Missing units** in the final numerical answer (ΔT must be in °C, P_v in W/m³). Examiners deduct **½ mark** per missing unit. > 2. **Confusing dielectric constant ε′ with loss factor ε″** — many students interchange them. Remember: **ε′ = storage**, **ε″ = loss (heat)**. > 3. **Skipping the rms factor** in the P_v equation — board questions explicitly test whether you used $E_{rms}$ or $E_0$. Use $E_{rms}$ unless told peak amplitude. > 4. **Mixing up 915 MHz with 2450 MHz penetration depths** — examiners expect the student to know that *lower frequency → deeper penetration*. > 5. **Forgetting 65 % absorption in numericals** — the absorption factor in the problem must be applied; a common one-mark loss. ## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist - Microwave (MW) frequency = **300 MHz – 300 GHz**; Radio Frequency (RF) = **1 – 100 MHz**. - Industrial ISM bands in food: **27.12 MHz (RF), 896/915 MHz, 2450 MHz (MW)**. - Two heating mechanisms: **dipolar rotation** and **ionic conduction**. - Volumetric heating — heat generated *inside* the food, not from outside. - Volumetric power density: $P_v = 2\pi f \, \varepsilon_0 \, \varepsilon'' \, E_{rms}^{2} = 55.61 \times 10^{-12} f \varepsilon'' E_{rms}^{2}$ (W/m³). - Penetration depth: $d = \dfrac{\lambda_0 \sqrt{\varepsilon'}}{2\pi \, \varepsilon''}$; **decreases with frequency and ε″**. - 2450 MHz → 1 – 3 cm penetration (domestic / thin products). - 915 MHz → 8 – 10 cm penetration (industrial thick products). - 27.12 MHz RF → 20 – 100+ cm penetration (bulk powders, post-bake drying). - Loss tangent: $\tan \delta = \varepsilon''/\varepsilon'$. - Continuous MW systems use **magnetrons + waveguides + applicator cavities**; RF systems use **capacitive electrodes**. - Applications: tempering, pasteurisation, blanching, baking, drying, thawing, cooking, sterilisation. - Advantages: speed, energy efficiency, nutrient retention, no surface over-cook. - Limitations: limited penetration, uneven heating at edges, capital cost, regulatory ceiling on leakage (< 5 mW/cm² at 5 cm).

topics.title: Ohmic Heating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT525 - NOVEL FOOD PROCESSING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Ohmic Heating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ohmic Heating (OH)** — also called **Joule Heating**, **Electroconductive Heating (ECH)**, or **Electrical Resistance Heating** — is an advanced thermal food processing technique in which an alternating electric current (typically **50 Hz – 10 MHz**) is passed directly through a food material that itself acts as an electrical resistor. Heat is generated *volumetrically* within the food matrix as a result of internal ionic migration and the natural electrical resistance of the product, in strict accordance with **Joule's First Law**.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, Ohmic Heating is classified under the family of **"Volumetric / Internal Heating Technologies"** alongside Microwave and Radio-Frequency (RF) heating. It is unique in that the food itself completes the electrical circuit, eliminating the requirement for any heat-transfer surface.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> Think of Ohmic Heating as **"boiling water using the water itself as the heating coil."**

Imagine you fill a long glass tube with a salty lemon solution (an electrolyte). You dip two stainless-steel rods at each end of the tube and connect them to a domestic AC supply. The lemon water itself begins to warm up uniformly — *not* from the electrodes, but from *inside* the entire volume. This is exactly what happens inside an Ohmic Heater. The current must "push" through the food, and because the food has finite electrical resistance, this pushing dissipates energy as heat throughout the bulk — even in solid food particles.

## 1.3 Key Engineering Metrics

- **Operating Frequency:** 50 Hz – 60 Hz (mains) up to **10 MHz** (industrial)
- **Voltage Gradient:** Typically **50 – 500 V/cm** across electrodes
- **Current Density:** Controlled between **1 – 10 A/cm²**
- **Electrical Conductivity of Food:** **0.01 – 10 S/m** (must be > 0.001 S/m for effective heating)

## 1.4 Why Ohmic Heating Matters in Food Engineering

> [!NOTE]
> Conventional heating requires thermal energy to **travel** from a hot surface into the food center (a slow conduction process). Ohmic heating generates heat *in situ* — meaning **time, energy, and quality loss due to surface overheating are drastically reduced.** This is the principal reason it is preferred for heat-sensitive, particulate-laden, and high-viscosity foods.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Governing Physical Principle — Joule's First Law

The fundamental principle rests on **Joule's Law of Electrical Heating**. When a current $I$ flows through a resistor $R$ for time $t$, the heat generated $Q$ is given by:

$$Q = I^{2} \, R \, t$$

This relationship is the *cornerstone* of all Ohmic Heating derivations.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

1. **Food Placement in Electrode Gap:** The food (liquid + particulates) is placed between two parallel metallic electrodes separated by a distance $L$.
2. **AC Field Application:** An alternating voltage $V$ is applied. The frequency is selected to prevent electrolysis (minimum 50 Hz).
3. **Ionic Conduction:** Dissolved salts and ionic species within the food migrate under the electric field, carrying charge.
4. **Resistive Dissipation:** Ions collide with water molecules and the food matrix, transferring kinetic energy → manifests as **heat**.
5. **Volumetric Generation:** Because every infinitesimal volume element of food has resistance, *heat is generated simultaneously throughout the entire bulk*.
6. **Uniform Temperature Rise:** Provided electrical conductivity $\sigma$ is uniform, the temperature rise is spatially uniform.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates **every equation** you are likely to need in a KTU ESE question on Ohmic Heating.

| # | Equation | Meaning of Symbols | Typical Units | Engineering Use |
|---|----------|--------------------|---------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $Q = I^{2} R t$ | Heat generated (Joule's Law) | $J$ | Basic heating power calculation |
| 2 | $Q = \dfrac{V^{2} t}{R}$ | Heat generated (voltage form) | $J$ | When $V$ and $R$ are known |
| 3 | $P = V I = I^{2} R = \dfrac{V^{2}}{R}$ | Instantaneous electrical power | $W$ | Power requirement for heater |
| 4 | $E = \dfrac{V}{d}$ | Electric field intensity | $V/m$ | Field strength across electrodes |
| 5 | $J = \dfrac{I}{A}$ | Current density | $A/m^{2}$ | Electrode loading |
| 6 | $\sigma = \dfrac{1}{\rho_{e}} = \dfrac{L}{R \, A}$ | Electrical conductivity of food | $S/m$ | Material property |
| 7 | $q = \sigma \, E^{2}$ | Volumetric heat generation rate | $W/m^{3}$ | Energy balance calculations |
| 8 | $\dfrac{dT}{dt} = \dfrac{\sigma \, E^{2}}{\rho \, C_{p}}$ | Instantaneous temperature rise rate | $K/s$ | Process design |
| 9 | $R = \rho_{e} \, \dfrac{L}{A}$ | Resistance of food column | $\Omega$ | Geometric design |
| 10 | $\sigma = \sigma_{0} \left[ 1 + m(T - T_{0}) \right]$ | Temperature-dependent conductivity | $S/m$ | Real product behaviour |
| 11 | $\eta = \dfrac{Q_{useful}}{Q_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Thermal/electrical efficiency | $\%$ | Performance evaluation |
| 12 | $t = \dfrac{\rho \, C_{p} \, \Delta T}{\sigma \, E^{2}}$ | Time to achieve a temperature rise | $s$ | Direct process time |

> [!NOTE]
> **Symbols Glossary:** $V$ = voltage, $I$ = current, $R$ = resistance, $t$ = time, $d$ = electrode gap, $A$ = electrode area, $L$ = length of food column, $\rho$ = food density, $C_{p}$ = specific heat, $T$ = temperature, $m$ = temperature coefficient, $\rho_{e}$ = electrical resistivity, $\eta$ = efficiency.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Continuous in-line pasteurization** of liquid eggs, fruit pulps, and milk.
- **Aseptic processing of particulate foods** (soups, stews) where sterilization of solid pieces is otherwise slow.
- **Pre-heating** for drying, evaporation, and extrusion.
- **Blanching** of vegetables without leaching losses.
- **Waste-to-energy** validation in biorefineries (heating biomass slurries).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Volumetric Heat Generation Rate

**Starting premise:** A cylindrical volume of food of cross-sectional area $A$ and length $L$ is placed between two parallel electrodes across which a voltage $V$ is applied.

**Step 1 — Electric Field Magnitude**

The electric field $E$ is defined as the voltage drop per unit length of the conducting medium:

$$E = \dfrac{V}{L}$$

*Logical conversion:* Since $V$ is applied uniformly across the entire gap of length $L$, the field is uniform and given by the ratio of potential difference to distance.

**Step 2 — Current Density from Ohm's Law (point form)**

For a homogeneous conductor, the current density $\vec{J}$ is given by the *point form* of Ohm's law:

$$\vec{J} = \sigma \, \vec{E}$$

*Logical conversion:* The current per unit area flowing through a conductor is directly proportional to the applied field and the conductivity of the medium.

**Step 3 — Magnitude of Current Density**

Since $E$ and $J$ are co-linear in this uniform field:

$$J = \sigma \, E$$

**Step 4 — Power Dissipated Per Unit Volume (q)**

The power dissipated per unit volume in a conductor carrying current density $J$ under field $E$ is:

$$q = \vec{J} \cdot \vec{E}$$

Substituting the magnitude form:

$$q = (\sigma E) \cdot E = \sigma E^{2}$$

$$\boxed{\,q = \sigma E^{2}\,} \quad \left[ \dfrac{W}{m^{3}} \right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the master equation of Ohmic Heating.** It tells us heat generation per unit volume is proportional to conductivity AND the *square* of the electric field — so doubling the voltage quadruples the heating rate.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Instantaneous Rate of Temperature Rise

**Step 1 — Total heat generated in a differential volume $dV$ in time $dt$:**

$$dQ = q \, dV \, dt = \sigma E^{2} \, dV \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Heat required to raise temperature of that volume by $dT$:**

$$dQ = m \, C_{p} \, dT = (\rho \, dV) \, C_{p} \, dT$$

*Logical conversion:* Mass equals density times volume, and the heat required to raise temperature by $dT$ is $m C_{p} dT$.

**Step 3 — Equate the two expressions (energy conservation):**

$$\sigma E^{2} \, dV \, dt = \rho \, C_{p} \, dV \, dT$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $dV$ from both sides:**

$$\sigma E^{2} \, dt = \rho \, C_{p} \, dT$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the temperature rise rate:**

$$\dfrac{dT}{dt} = \dfrac{\sigma E^{2}}{\rho \, C_{p}}$$

$$\boxed{\,\dfrac{dT}{dt} = \dfrac{\sigma \, E^{2}}{\rho \, C_{p}}\,} \quad \left[ \dfrac{K}{s} \right]$$

## 3.3 Derivation III — Time Required to Achieve a Given Temperature Rise

Rearranging the previous result and integrating from initial temperature $T_{i}$ to final temperature $T_{f}$:

$$\int_{0}^{t} dt = \int_{T_{i}}^{T_{f}} \dfrac{\rho C_{p}}{\sigma E^{2}} \, dT$$

Assuming $\sigma$ is approximately constant over the temperature range:

$$t = \dfrac{\rho \, C_{p} \, (T_{f} - T_{i})}{\sigma \, E^{2}}$$

$$\boxed{\,t = \dfrac{\rho \, C_{p} \, \Delta T}{\sigma \, E^{2}}\,} \quad [s]$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Typical Numerical Values

| Food Product | $\sigma$ (S/m) | $\rho$ (kg/m³) | $C_{p}$ (kJ/kg·K) | Typical $E$ (V/m) | $dT/dt$ (K/s) |
|--------------|----------------|----------------|---------------------|--------------------|----------------|
| Milk | 0.42 | 1030 | 3.93 | 1000 | 10.4 |
| Orange juice | 0.40 | 1040 | 3.90 | 1000 | 9.9 |
| Beef (ground) | 0.50 | 1050 | 3.50 | 1000 | 13.6 |
| Carrot (solid) | 0.05 | 880 | 3.85 | 1000 | 1.5 |
| Tomato soup | 1.20 | 1020 | 3.95 | 1000 | 29.7 |

> [!NOTE]
> The table highlights a **practical engineering reality** — low-conductivity solids (e.g., raw carrot) heat slowly under Ohmic Heating, while high-conductivity liquids and tissue-rich foods heat extremely fast.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AC Power Supply 50 Hz to 10 MHz] --> B[Step up Transformer]
    B --> C[Voltage Stabilizer and Control Unit]
    C --> D[Current Limiter]
    D --> E1[Electrode 1 Stainless Steel or Titanium]
    D --> E2[Electrode 2 Stainless Steel or Titanium]
    E1 --> F1[Food Chamber with Electrode Gap L]
    F1 --> G1[Food Matrix with Electrical Conductivity sigma]
    G1 --> H1[Volumetric Heat Generation sigma times E squared]
    E2 --> F1
    H1 --> I1[Uniform Temperature Rise dT by dt equals sigma E squared over rho Cp]
    I1 --> J1[Outlet for Processed Food]
    I1 --> K1[Temperature and Conductivity Sensors]
    K1 --> C
    H1 --> L1[Non Uniform Heating Region if sigma varies]
    L1 --> M1[Process Engineer Adjustment via Feedback Loop]
    M1 --> C
```

## 4.2 Schematic Description of the System

The system is composed of **four major sub-units**:

1. **Power Source & Conditioning Unit:** AC mains → step-up transformer → voltage regulator → current limiter. Maintains the desired voltage gradient $E = V/d$ and prevents runaway currents.
2. **Electrode Assembly:** Two flat, parallel electrodes (usually **stainless steel SS-316** or **titanium** to resist corrosion and electrolytic pitting) separated by gap $d$. The food flows through the inter-electrode space.
3. **Treatment Chamber:** Houses the food; may be **batch-type** (static) or **continuous** (in-line, with screw or belt transport).
4. **Sensor & Feedback Loop:** Measures $T$, $I$, and $V$ in real time; feeds the controller to modulate power and prevent localized over-heating ("hot spots").

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Design Insight:** If electrical conductivity $\sigma$ varies spatially within the food (e.g., a carrot piece in a salty broth), the low-$\sigma$ region heats *less* and the high-$\sigma$ region heats *more* — this is called the **"conductivity-matching problem"** and is the single most researched challenge in Ohmic Heating of particulate foods.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define Ohmic Heating. Mention any two of its advantages over conventional thermal processing.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Ohmic Heating is a thermal food processing method in which heat is generated *within* the food by passing an alternating electric current through it, exploiting the food's own electrical resistance in accordance with Joule's Law ($Q = I^{2}Rt$).

**Two Advantages:**
1. **Volumetric & Uniform Heating:** Heat is generated throughout the food bulk simultaneously, eliminating slow surface-to-centre conduction gradients.
2. **High Thermal Efficiency (≈ 90 %):** Almost all electrical energy is converted to heat inside the food; there are no hot walls or heat-transfer surfaces.
3. **Minimal Quality Loss:** Rapid heating and absence of surface overheating preserve colour, flavour, and nutrients.
4. **Suitability for Particulates:** Enables sterilization of solid–liquid mixtures (e.g., soups with vegetable pieces).

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**State Joule's Law of Heating. Write the expression for volumetric heat generation rate in Ohmic Heating.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Joule's Law:** The heat $Q$ developed in a conductor of resistance $R$ carrying current $I$ for time $t$ is:
>
> $Q = I^{2} R t$

**Volumetric Heat Generation Rate:**

$$q = \sigma \, E^{2} \quad \left[ \dfrac{W}{m^{3}} \right]$$

where $\sigma$ = electrical conductivity of the food (S/m) and $E$ = electric field intensity (V/m).

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme, Part B questions carry internal choice. Two independent alternatives are provided below.

---

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **rate of temperature rise** in a food undergoing Ohmic Heating. Clearly state the assumptions used. (7 marks)

**(b)** A **tomato soup** with $\sigma = 1.2 \text{ S/m}$, $\rho = 1020 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$, and $C_{p} = 3.95 \text{ kJ/kg·K}$ is heated in an Ohmic Heater with electrode gap $d = 0.02 \text{ m}$ and applied voltage $V = 230 \text{ V}$. Calculate:
1. The electric field intensity $E$. (2 marks)
2. The volumetric heat generation rate $q$. (2 marks)
3. The time required to raise the temperature from **30 °C to 90 °C**. (3 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Assumptions:**
1. Uniform electrical conductivity throughout the food.
2. No heat loss to surroundings (adiabatic).
3. Constant $\rho$ and $C_{p}$ over the temperature range.
4. Uniform electric field (parallel electrodes, no edge effects).

**Derivation:**

Heat generated in volume $dV$ in time $dt$:

$$dQ = q \, dV \, dt = \sigma E^{2} \, dV \, dt$$

Heat required for a temperature rise $dT$ of the same mass:

$$dQ = m \, C_{p} \, dT = (\rho \, dV) \, C_{p} \, dT$$

Equating both and cancelling $dV$:

$$\sigma E^{2} \, dt = \rho C_{p} \, dT$$

$$\boxed{\,\dfrac{dT}{dt} = \dfrac{\sigma E^{2}}{\rho C_{p}}\,}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** $\sigma = 1.2 \text{ S/m}$, $\rho = 1020 \text{ kg/m}^{3}$, $C_{p} = 3950 \text{ J/kg·K}$, $d = 0.02 \text{ m}$, $V = 230 \text{ V}$, $\Delta T = 90 - 30 = 60 \text{ K}$.

**1. Electric field intensity $E$:**

$$E = \dfrac{V}{d} = \dfrac{230}{0.02} = 11{,}500 \text{ V/m}$$

**2. Volumetric heat generation rate $q$:**

$$q = \sigma E^{2} = 1.2 \times (11{,}500)^{2} = 1.2 \times 1.3225 \times 10^{8}$$

$$\boxed{q = 1.587 \times 10^{8} \text{ W/m}^{3}}$$

**3. Time to heat from 30 °C → 90 °C:**

$$t = \dfrac{\rho \, C_{p} \, \Delta T}{\sigma \, E^{2}} = \dfrac{1020 \times 3950 \times 60}{1.2 \times (11{,}500)^{2}}$$

Computing numerator:

$$1020 \times 3950 \times 60 = 2.4174 \times 10^{8}$$

Computing denominator:

$$1.2 \times 1.3225 \times 10^{8} = 1.587 \times 10^{8}$$

Therefore:

$$t = \dfrac{2.4174 \times 10^{8}}{1.587 \times 10^{8}} = 1.523 \text{ s}$$

$$\boxed{t \approx 1.52 \text{ seconds}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This extraordinarily short heating time is what makes Ohmic Heating attractive — a comparable conventional heat-exchanger would take 30–60 seconds with significantly more surface fouling.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the **working principle of a continuous Ohmic Heating system** with the help of a labelled block diagram. List the materials used for electrodes and the reason for their selection. (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss the **key factors affecting Ohmic Heating** of food and explain the **"conductivity-matching"** problem in particulate foods. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Working Principle:**

In a continuous Ohmic Heating system, the food (liquid + particulates) is pumped through a tubular or rectangular chamber fitted with **two or more pairs of electrodes** along its length. An AC voltage is applied across the electrodes. As the food flows, current passes through it, and Joule heating raises its temperature rapidly and uniformly.

The block sequence is:

**AC mains → Step-up transformer → Voltage regulator → Current limiter → Electrode pair → Food flow → Outlet.**

The power is controlled by feedback from **temperature sensors** placed downstream of each electrode pair. Multiple electrode pairs allow **zonal control**, where each section of the heater is set to a different power level to achieve a desired temperature profile.

**Electrode Materials & Reasons:**

| Material | Reason for Selection |
|----------|----------------------|
| **Stainless Steel (SS-316)** | Corrosion-resistant, food-grade, mechanically strong, low cost |
| **Titanium** | Excellent resistance to electrolytic pitting; ideal for salty/acidic foods |
| **Graphite** | Chemically inert, cheap, but mechanically fragile and porous |
| **Platinum-coated Titanium** | Used in highly corrosive or research-grade applications |

> [!NOTE]
> The primary selection criterion is **resistance to electrolytic corrosion** at the food-electrode interface. The frequency (≥ 50 Hz) is chosen above the electrolysis threshold to prevent gas generation (H₂ / O₂) at the electrodes.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Key Factors Affecting Ohmic Heating:**

1. **Electrical Conductivity ($\sigma$) of the food:** Higher $\sigma$ → more current → more heat. $\sigma$ is itself temperature-dependent.
2. **Electric Field Intensity ($E$):** Heat generation scales as $E^{2}$. Doubling voltage quadruples heating.
3. **Frequency of AC Supply:** Low frequencies (< 25 Hz) cause electrolysis; very high frequencies (> 10 MHz) cause radio interference. **50 Hz – 10 MHz** is the safe window.
4. **Food Composition:** Salt, ionic content, and moisture enhance $\sigma$ and hence heating.
5. **Particle Size and Shape:** Affects uniformity of heating in particulate foods.
6. **Electrode Geometry and Gap:** Influences field uniformity and total power.
7. **Flow Rate (in continuous systems):** Determines residence time and final temperature.

**Conductivity-Matching Problem:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In a mixture of solids and liquids (e.g., vegetables in broth), the solid and liquid phases often have **different electrical conductivities**. During heating, the **higher-$\sigma$ phase heats faster**, while the **lower-$\sigma$ phase lags behind.** If the conductivity difference is large, the liquid may over-process (loss of quality) before the solid reaches the required sterilization temperature — defeating the purpose of the heat treatment.

**Engineering Solution:** Match the conductivities by adjusting the **salt content** of the liquid carrier, or use **pre-blanching** to bring the solid's $\sigma$ closer to the liquid's. Research in this area is ongoing and is a key topic in KTU 2024 Module 3.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in KTU exams:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $C_{p}$ from kJ/kg·K to J/kg·K** in numerical problems — this alone causes a 1–2 mark deduction.
> 2. **Using RMS vs peak voltage incorrectly** — KTU expects the **RMS value** unless explicitly stated.
> 3. **Mixing up $E = V/d$ and $E = V/L$** — both are valid depending on the chosen reference; be consistent.
> 4. **Omitting the units in the final answer** — KTU strictly penalizes unitless numerical answers.
> 5. **Stating "Ohmic heating uses microwaves"** — this is a **fatal conceptual error**. Ohmic and Microwave are independent technologies.
> 6. **Skipping the "frequency > 50 Hz" justification** in derivations involving electrolysis prevention.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Ohmic Heating (Module 3):**

- ✅ Ohmic Heating = **internal / volumetric heating** using the food's own electrical resistance.
- ✅ Governed by **Joule's First Law:** $Q = I^{2} R t$.
- ✅ Frequency: **50 Hz – 10 MHz** (avoid electrolysis).
- ✅ Master equation: $q = \sigma E^{2}$ (volumetric heat generation, W/m³).
- ✅ Temperature rise rate: $\dfrac{dT}{dt} = \dfrac{\sigma E^{2}}{\rho C_{p}}$.
- ✅ Heating time: $t = \dfrac{\rho C_{p} \Delta T}{\sigma E^{2}}$.
- ✅ Typical conductivities of foods: **0.01 – 10 S/m**.
- ✅ **Electrode materials:** SS-316, Titanium, Graphite (corrosion resistance is key).
- ✅ **Advantages:** uniform, rapid, high efficiency, suitable for particulates, no surface fouling.
- ✅ **Limitations:** requires sufficient $\sigma$ (> 0.001 S/m); conductivity-mismatch in solids; electrolytic risk at low frequency; high capital cost.
- ✅ **Applications:** pasteurization of milk, juice, soups; sterilization of particulate foods; pre-heating for drying & evaporation; blanching of vegetables.
- ✅ **Conductivity-matching problem** is the central research challenge for particulate Ohmic Heating.
- ✅ Compare/contrast with **Microwave Heating** (electromagnetic vs. ionic conduction).
- ✅ Compare/contrast with **Conventional Heating** (surface heat transfer vs. volumetric generation).
- ✅ Always carry **units** in numerical answers; always state **assumptions** in derivations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Infrared (IR) Heating
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT525 - NOVEL FOOD PROCESSING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Infrared (IR) Heating

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Infrared (IR) Heating** is an advanced thermal food processing technique that employs **electromagnetic radiation** in the wavelength range of **0.7 µm to 1000 µm** (often operationally confined to **0.7 µm – 100 µm** for thermal food applications) to transfer energy directly to the surface and near-surface layers of a food material. The energy is absorbed by the food matrix at the molecular level — primarily by exciting vibrational, rotational, and translational modes of water, proteins, carbohydrates, and lipids — and is subsequently converted into **sensible heat (thermal energy)** without requiring a heated intermediate fluid medium.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard Reference):**
> *Infrared heating is a non-ionizing electromagnetic radiation-based thermal processing method in which radiant energy is transferred from a hot emitter surface to a food product through electromagnetic waves, where it is absorbed, reflected, or transmitted depending on the optical properties of the food material.*

## 1.2 Classification of Infrared Radiation

The infrared band of the electromagnetic spectrum is conventionally subdivided into three regions based on wavelength:

| Region | Wavelength Range (µm) | Temperature of Peak Emitter (K) | Typical Food Application |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **Near-Infrared (NIR)** | **0.7 – 1.4** | **> 2070** | Surface sterilization, baking crust formation |
| **Short-Wave IR (SWIR)** | **1.4 – 3.0** | **966 – 2070** | High-speed drying, paint curing |
| **Mid-Wave IR (MWIR)** | **3.0 – 8.0** | **362 – 966** | Baking, drying of vegetables |
| **Long-Wave / Far-IR (FIR)** | **8.0 – 1000** | **< 362** | Dehydration, tempering of frozen foods |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sun-Bathed Sand" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: Standing on a Sunlit Beach**
>
> Imagine walking barefoot on a beach on a bright summer afternoon. The **sand gets burning hot** within minutes, but the **air above it remains comparatively cool**. This is precisely the principle of **Infrared (IR) Heating**:
>
> - The **Sun** acts as the **IR emitter** (a high-temperature blackbody radiator).
> - The **electromagnetic waves** travel through the cool, transparent air **without heating it** (no medium-heating losses).
> - The **sand** (analogous to a food product) **absorbs** this radiant energy directly, becoming hot at the surface and then conducting heat inward.
>
> This is fundamentally different from **convection** (where hot air must first touch the food) or **conduction** (where a hot pan transfers heat by contact). **IR heating is a "light-to-heat" conversion** that bypasses the need for an intermediate hot medium — making it **faster, more energy-efficient, and less destructive to heat-sensitive nutrients** in many cases.

## 1.4 Distinguishing IR Heating from Other Novel Methods

- **IR vs. Microwave (MW) Heating:** MW operates at **915 MHz / 2450 MHz** (longer wavelength, deeper penetration ~ 1–10 cm in food) by **dielectric heating of polar molecules (water)**. IR is purely **surface/subsurface radiant heating** with penetration depths of typically **0.1 – 10 mm**.
- **IR vs. Ohmic Heating:** Ohmic uses **electrical current (Joule effect)** passing *through* the food. IR uses **electromagnetic waves** that *strike* the food surface.
- **IR vs. Radio-Frequency (RF) Heating:** RF (~ 27 MHz) penetrates even deeper than MW. IR remains a **surface-dominated, high heat-flux technology**.

> [!TIP]
> **Key KTU Takeaway:** IR is a **"surface-dominant, rapid-response, energy-efficient"** thermal technology — best suited for operations requiring **high surface temperatures with short residence times** (e.g., crust browning, moisture removal, surface pasteurization).

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Physics of Infrared Radiation

IR heating is governed by the principles of **thermal radiation**, an emission phenomenon where a body emits electromagnetic energy as a function of its absolute temperature. Three fundamental laws form the theoretical backbone:

### 2.1.1 Planck's Law of Blackbody Radiation

A **blackbody** is an idealized perfect absorber and emitter. Planck's law gives the **spectral emissive power** $E_{\lambda}$ (W/m²·µm) as a function of wavelength $\lambda$ and absolute temperature $T$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\lambda}(\lambda, T) &= \frac{2 \pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{hc / \lambda k T} - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $h$ = **Planck's constant = 6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
- $c$ = **Speed of light = 2.998 × 10⁸ m/s**
- $k$ = **Boltzmann constant = 1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- $\lambda$ = Wavelength (m)
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)

### 2.1.2 Wien's Displacement Law (Peak Wavelength)

The wavelength at which a blackbody emits **maximum radiation intensity** is inversely proportional to its absolute temperature:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{\max} \cdot T &= 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \;\; \text{m·K} \;\; \approx 2898 \;\; \mu\text{m·K}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** *The hotter the emitter, the shorter the peak wavelength.* A 1000 K emitter peaks near 2.9 µm (mid-IR), while a 5000 K source peaks near 0.58 µm (visible light). This is why **gas-fired IR burners (1500–2500 K)** emit predominantly in the **near-IR**, while **electric ceramic emitters (700–1000 K)** emit in the **far-IR**.

### 2.1.3 Stefan-Boltzmann Law (Total Radiant Exitance)

Integrating Planck's equation over all wavelengths gives the **total emissive power** of a blackbody:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{b} &= \sigma T^{4} \;\;\; \text{W/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **real (gray) body** with emissivity $\varepsilon$ (dimensionless, $0 \le \varepsilon \le 1$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $\sigma$ = **Stefan-Boltzmann constant = 5.67 × 10⁻⁸ W/(m²·K⁴)**.

### 2.1.4 Kirchhoff's Law (Absorption = Emission at Thermal Equilibrium)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\varepsilon_{\lambda} &= \alpha_{\lambda}
\end{aligned}
$$

> A surface that is a **good absorber at a given wavelength is also a good emitter at the same wavelength**. This explains why **wet/glossy foods (high $\alpha$ at water absorption bands) are excellent IR absorbers**, and also why they re-radiate heat efficiently back from their surface.

## 2.2 Net Radiative Heat Transfer Between Two Surfaces

The **net heat flux** $q_{net}$ between an IR emitter surface ($T_s$) and a food surface ($T_f$), both gray, parallel, and facing each other, is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{net} &= \frac{\sigma \left( T_{s}^{4} - T_{f}^{4} \right)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_{s}} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_{f}} - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

When $\varepsilon_s = \varepsilon_f = 1$ (blackbody limit), this reduces to $q_{net} = \sigma (T_s^4 - T_f^4)$.

## 2.3 Beer-Lambert Law (IR Penetration in Food)

Once IR radiation strikes the food, the **internal intensity** $I(x)$ decays exponentially with depth $x$ into the material:

$$
\begin{aligned}
I(x) &= I_{0} \cdot e^{-\mu x}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $I_0$ = Incident intensity at surface (W/m²)
- $\mu$ = **Absorption coefficient (m⁻¹)**, material-dependent
- $x$ = Depth into the food (m)

The **penetration depth** $\delta$ is defined as the depth at which intensity falls to $1/e$ (~ 36.8%) of $I_0$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta &= \frac{1}{\mu} \;\;\; \text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

Typical penetration depths in foods: **0.1 – 10 mm** (much shallower than microwave).

## 2.4 Energy Balance for IR-Heated Food

The temperature rise in a food undergoing IR heating follows:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m \, c_{p} \, \frac{dT}{dt} &= q_{abs} - q_{loss} - q_{evap}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $m$ = Mass of food (kg)
- $c_p$ = Specific heat capacity (kJ/kg·K)
- $q_{abs} = \eta \, A \, \varepsilon_f \, \sigma (T_s^4 - T_f^4)$ = Absorbed IR power (W)
- $q_{loss}$ = Combined convective + re-radiation loss (W)
- $q_{evap}$ = Latent heat loss from moisture evaporation (W)
- $\eta$ = Geometric/view-factor efficiency (dimensionless)
- $A$ = Exposed surface area (m²)

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Concept | Final Form | Primary Use | Typical Units |
|:-:|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Stefan-Boltzmann** | $E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$ | Total emissive power | W/m² |
| 2 | **Wien's Displacement** | $\lambda_{\max} = 2898 / T$ | Peak emission wavelength | µm (when T in K) |
| 3 | **Net Heat Flux** | $q_{net} = \sigma (T_s^4 - T_f^4) / [(1/\varepsilon_s) + (1/\varepsilon_f) - 1]$ | Emitter-to-food transfer | W/m² |
| 4 | **Beer-Lambert** | $I(x) = I_0 \, e^{-\mu x}$ | Internal intensity decay | W/m² |
| 5 | **Penetration Depth** | $\delta = 1/\mu$ | Effective heating depth | m |
| 6 | **Kirchhoff's Law** | $\varepsilon = \alpha$ | Absorptivity = emissivity | dimensionless |
| 7 | **Food Energy Balance** | $m c_p dT/dt = q_{abs} - q_{loss} - q_{evap}$ | Heating rate prediction | W |
| 8 | **Radiation View Factor** | $F_{s \to f}$ | Geometric coupling | dimensionless |
| 9 | **Mass Evaporation Rate** | $\dot{m}_{w} = q_{abs} / h_{fg}$ | Drying rate | kg/s |
| 10 | **Surface Energy Density** | $Q = \int q_{net} \, dt$ | Total energy supplied | J/m² |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** In the KTU exam, do not confuse the **emissivity of the food** (which is typically **0.85–0.95** for wet products) with the **emissivity of the emitter** (often **0.95–0.99** for ceramics, ~ 0.1 for polished metal). Use the **combined form** of the net heat-flux equation for full-mark credit.

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Food Industry:** Drying fruits/vegetables (IR + hot air hybrid), baking bread/crackers, surface pasteurization of spices, crust browning of pizzas, tempering of frozen meat.
- **Energy Efficiency:** Direct surface heating eliminates preheating of large air volumes → **30–70% energy savings** vs. conventional hot-air ovens in many cases.
- **Process Control:** IR lamps reach operating temperature in **< 1 second** → enables precise ON/OFF modulation (much faster than steam-heated systems).
- **Quality Impact:** Short exposure times preserve **heat-labile nutrients (Vitamin C, thiamine)** better than prolonged conventional heating.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation A — Stefan-Boltzmann Law from Planck's Law

**Goal:** Derive $E_b = \sigma T^4$ by integrating Planck's spectral emissive power over all wavelengths from $\lambda = 0$ to $\lambda \to \infty$.

### Step 1 — Write Planck's Spectral Emissive Power

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{\lambda}(\lambda, T) &= \frac{2 \pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda kT} - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

This expresses the energy emitted per unit wavelength interval per unit surface area per unit solid angle (after integration over $2\pi$ steradians, the factor $2\pi$ appears).

### Step 2 — Integrate Over All Wavelengths to Get Total Emissive Power

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{b}(T) &= \int_{0}^{\infty} E_{\lambda}(\lambda, T) \, d\lambda \\
&= \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{2 \pi h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda kT} - 1} \, d\lambda
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Change of Variable

Let $u = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda kT}$, so $\lambda = \dfrac{hc}{u kT}$ and $d\lambda = -\dfrac{hc}{u^{2} kT} \, du$.

Substituting and simplifying the limits ($\lambda: 0 \to \infty$ becomes $u: \infty \to 0$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{b}(T) &= \frac{2 \pi k^{4} T^{4}}{h^{3} c^{2}} \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{u^{3}}{e^{u} - 1} \, du
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Integral Using the Riemann Zeta Function

The integral has a known closed-form value:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{u^{3}}{e^{u} - 1} \, du &= \frac{\pi^{4}}{15} \approx 6.4939
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Substitute the Integral Value

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{b}(T) &= \frac{2 \pi k^{4} T^{4}}{h^{3} c^{2}} \cdot \frac{\pi^{4}}{15} \\
&= \frac{2 \pi^{5} k^{4}}{15 h^{3} c^{2}} \cdot T^{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 6 — Identify the Stefan-Boltzmann Constant

Define $\sigma = \dfrac{2 \pi^{5} k^{4}}{15 h^{3} c^{2}}$ and compute numerically:

- $k = 1.381 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K
- $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
- $c = 2.998 \times 10^{8}$ m/s

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma &= \frac{2 \pi^{5} (1.381 \times 10^{-23})^{4}}{15 (6.626 \times 10^{-34})^{3} (2.998 \times 10^{8})^{2}} \\
&\approx 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \;\;\; \text{W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K}^4)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Final Result

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{b}(T) &= \sigma T^{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is the **Stefan-Boltzmann Law** for a blackbody — the cornerstone of all IR-heating calculations.

---

## 3.2 Derivation B — Net IR Heat Transfer to a Food Surface

**Goal:** Derive the net heat flux received by a food surface of area $A_f$ and emissivity $\varepsilon_f$ from an emitter of area $A_s$ and emissivity $\varepsilon_s$ at temperatures $T_s$ and $T_f$ respectively.

### Step 1 — Write Energy Emitted by Each Surface (Per Unit Area)

The emitter radiates per unit area:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_s &= \varepsilon_s \sigma T_s^{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

The food surface (treated as a gray body at $T_f$) radiates back:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_f &= \varepsilon_f \sigma T_f^{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Apply View Factor and Radiation Network

In the **two-surface enclosure** (small parallel plates, no edge losses), the view factor $F_{sf} = 1$. Using the radiation network (analogous to electrical resistors in series):

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{net} &= \frac{A_s \sigma \left( T_s^{4} - T_f^{4} \right)}{\dfrac{1 - \varepsilon_s}{\varepsilon_s} + \dfrac{1}{F_{sf}} + \dfrac{1 - \varepsilon_f}{\varepsilon_f}}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Simplify for Large Parallel Surfaces ($A_s \approx A_f$)

With $A_s = A_f$ and $F_{sf} = 1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{net} &= A \cdot \frac{\sigma \left( T_s^{4} - T_f^{4} \right)}{\dfrac{1 - \varepsilon_s}{\varepsilon_s} + 1 + \dfrac{1 - \varepsilon_f}{\varepsilon_f}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Note that $\dfrac{1 - \varepsilon}{\varepsilon} = \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon} - 1$, so the denominator becomes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left( \frac{1}{\varepsilon_s} - 1 \right) + 1 + \left( \frac{1}{\varepsilon_f} - 1 \right) &= \frac{1}{\varepsilon_s} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_f} - 1
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Final Compact Form

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{net} &= A \cdot \frac{\sigma \left( T_s^{4} - T_f^{4} \right)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_s} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_f} - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 5 — Special Case: Blackbody Limit ($\varepsilon_s = \varepsilon_f = 1$)

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{net} &= A \cdot \sigma \left( T_s^{4} - T_f^{4} \right)
\end{aligned}
$$

> This form is the **most-tested equation** in KTU IR-heating problems.

---

## 3.3 Derivation C — Penetration Depth in a Moist Food

**Goal:** Compute the IR penetration depth in a food with 80% moisture content at $\lambda = 3 \, \mu$m.

### Step 1 — Recall the Absorption Coefficient of Water at 3 µm

From published optical-property data for liquid water (compiled in KTU-recommended food physics texts), the absorption coefficient at $\lambda = 3 \, \mu$m is approximately:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{water} &\approx 1000 \;\; \text{cm}^{-1} = 10^{5} \;\; \text{m}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 2 — Use a Mixing Rule for Moist Food

For a food with moisture fraction $w$ (mass basis) and assuming the absorption scales linearly with water content:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\mu_{food} &\approx w \cdot \mu_{water} = 0.80 \times 10^{5} = 8 \times 10^{4} \;\; \text{m}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Calculate the Penetration Depth

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta &= \frac{1}{\mu_{food}} = \frac{1}{8 \times 10^{4}} \;\; \text{m} \\
&= 1.25 \times 10^{-5} \;\; \text{m} \\
&= 12.5 \;\; \mu\text{m}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Physical Interpretation

> Only the topmost **~ 12.5 µm** of the food receives significant IR energy at $\lambda = 3 \, \mu$m. Beyond that, intensity has dropped to **1/e ≈ 36.8%**, and at $5\delta$ (~ 62.5 µm), intensity is only **0.67%** of $I_0$.
>
> **Conclusion:** Mid-IR at 3 µm is **extremely surface-localized** — ideal for **surface sterilization, crust formation, or rapid moisture evaporation** from the surface, but not for bulk heating.

### Step 5 — Comparison: Penetration at Near-IR (1 µm)

At $\lambda = 1 \, \mu$m, $\mu_{water} \approx 1 \, \text{cm}^{-1} = 100 \, \text{m}^{-1}$, so:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\delta_{NIR} &= \frac{1}{0.80 \times 100} = 0.0125 \;\; \text{m} = 12.5 \;\; \text{mm}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Near-IR penetrates **1000× deeper** than mid-IR in the same food — explaining why **NIR** is used for **deep drying, thawing, and tempering**, while **mid/far-IR** is reserved for **surface treatment**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing IR Configuration for a Food Application

| Food Application | Recommended IR Type | Peak $\lambda$ (µm) | Emitter Temp (K) | Penetration Need |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| **Bread crust browning** | Near-IR + Mid-IR | 0.9 – 2.5 | 1500 – 2500 | Shallow (1–3 mm) |
| **Snack pellet cooking** | Far-IR | 3 – 6 | 700 – 900 | Moderate (3–5 mm) |
| **Vegetable dehydration** | Mid-IR + Convection | 2.5 – 4 | 900 – 1200 | Moderate (3–6 mm) |
| **Spice pasteurization** | Far-IR | 4 – 8 | 500 – 800 | Very shallow (< 1 mm) |
| **Frozen-meat tempering** | Near-IR | 0.8 – 1.2 | 2000 – 3000 | Deep (5–10 mm) |
| **Pizza oven** | Near-IR (gas) | 1.0 – 1.7 | 1700 – 2900 | Shallow + bulk conduction |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb:** Select the emitter temperature such that $\lambda_{\max}$ corresponds to a **strong absorption band of the target food component**. For water-rich foods, target **2.9 – 3.1 µm** (water O–H stretch). For oil/fat-rich foods, target **3.4 – 3.5 µm** (C–H stretch).

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an IR Food Processing System

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **interactions and energy flow** within a typical industrial IR food processing line (e.g., a snack-pellet cooking tunnel). It is intentionally a **functional block architecture** (not a physical engineering drawing) so that it compiles reliably across all Mermaid renderers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    PWR[Step 1 Power Supply 415V AC Three Phase] --> CTRL[Step 2 Control Unit PLC and Thyristor]
    GAS[Step 3 Gas Supply Line LPG or Natural Gas] --> MIX[Step 4 Gas Mixing Manifold]
    MIX --> BURN[Step 5 IR Burner Module]
    PWR --> EMTR[Step 6 Electric IR Emitter Ceramic or Tungsten]
    BURN --> RAD[Step 7 Radiant IR Field Wavelength 0.7 to 8 um]
    EMTR --> RAD
    RAD --> FOOD[Step 8 Food Product on Conveyor]
    FOOD --> ABS[Step 9 Surface Absorption Molecular Vibrational Excitation]
    ABS --> CONV[Step 10 Internal Conduction into Food Bulk]
    ABS --> EVAP[Step 11 Moisture Evaporation from Surface]
    CONV --> QSEN[Step 12 Quality Sensors Moisture Color Temperature]
    EVAP --> EXH[Step 13 Exhaust Hood and Vapor Removal]
    QSEN --> FBK[Step 14 Feedback Signal to Controller]
    FBK --> CTRL
    CTRL --> PWR
    CTRL --> MIX
    EXH --> ATM[Step 15 Atmosphere Wasted Vapor]
```

> **Reading the Diagram:**
> - **Steps 1–6** form the **energy-generation subsystem** (either electrical or gas-fired).
> - **Steps 7–11** represent the **radiation-absorption-and-conversion subsystem** (the heart of IR heating).
> - **Steps 12–15** complete the **closed-loop control subsystem**, ensuring product quality and energy efficiency.
> - The **feedback loop** (Steps 12 → 14 → 2/3) is what makes modern IR systems **adaptive and energy-optimized** — a key KTU conceptual highlight.

## 4.2 Simplified Energy-Flow Schematic (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Electrical Energy Input kW] --> B[IR Emitter Tungsten or Ceramic]
    G[Chemical Energy LPG Combustion] --> B2[Gas Burner Mesh]
    B2 --> C[IR Radiation Field]
    B --> C
    C --> D[Food Surface Absorptivity epsilon_f]
    D --> E{Surface Partition}
    E -->|Reflected| R[Reflected IR Lost]
    E -->|Transmitted| T[Transmitted IR Through Thin Layers]
    E -->|Absorbed| S[Absorbed IR q_abs]
    S --> F[Sensible Heat Temperature Rise]
    S --> M[Moisture Evaporation Latent Heat]
    F --> P[Product Quality and Cook Value]
    M --> P
```

> **Conceptual Reading:**
> - The **partition of incident IR** into Absorbed / Reflected / Transmitted (governed by $\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$) is the **first design consideration** for any IR application.
> - Most foods have **$\alpha > 0.85$** (high absorptivity), so the **reflected + transmitted fraction is small** — making IR highly energy-efficient.
