# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define infrared heating. State the wavelength range of infrared radiation used in food processing.**

**Model Answer:**

Infrared (IR) heating is a thermal food processing technique in which **electromagnetic radiation in the wavelength range of 0.7 µm to 1000 µm** is emitted by a high-temperature source, transmitted through the surrounding atmosphere (without appreciably heating it), and **absorbed at the food surface**, where it is converted into sensible heat via molecular vibrational excitation.

For food-processing applications, the practically useful range is **0.7 µm to 100 µm**, subdivided into:
- **Near-IR:** 0.7 – 1.4 µm
- **Mid-IR:** 1.4 – 8.0 µm
- **Far-IR:** 8.0 – 100 µm

**Key point:** IR heating requires **no heated intermediate medium** (unlike convection), giving it a unique advantage in terms of speed and energy efficiency.

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**State and explain Wien's Displacement Law. Mention its significance in IR food processing.**

**Model Answer:**

**Statement:** Wien's Displacement Law states that the wavelength at which a blackbody emits maximum radiation is inversely proportional to its absolute temperature:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_{\max} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3} \;\;\; \text{m·K}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Significance in Food Processing:**
- It allows the **designer to select the emitter temperature** that will deliver maximum IR energy at the wavelength of **strongest absorption by the food**.
- Example: For peak emission at $\lambda = 3 \, \mu\text{m}$ (a strong water-absorption band), the ideal emitter temperature is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T &= \frac{2898 \, \mu\text{m·K}}{3 \, \mu\text{m}} = 966 \;\;\; \text{K} \;\; (\approx 693^{\circ}\text{C})
\end{aligned}
$$

- A **higher emitter temperature** shifts $\lambda_{\max}$ to the **near-IR** → greater penetration, useful for deep drying/tempering.
- A **lower emitter temperature** shifts $\lambda_{\max}$ to the **far-IR** → shallower penetration, ideal for surface pasteurization.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the mechanism of infrared heating of foods with the help of a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of water as the principal absorbing component.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Mechanism of IR Heating (4 marks):**

Infrared heating proceeds through the following sequential steps:

1. **Generation:** An IR emitter (electric ceramic, tungsten-halogen, or gas-fired metal-fibre burner) is energized to a high temperature (**700 – 2500 K**). At this temperature, it emits a continuous spectrum of electromagnetic radiation, with $\lambda_{\max}$ determined by Wien's law.
2. **Transmission:** The IR waves propagate through the surrounding air with **minimal absorption** (air is largely transparent in 0.7 – 8 µm range), so no energy is wasted on heating the bulk atmosphere.
3. **Interaction at Food Surface:** When IR strikes the food surface, three phenomena occur simultaneously, governed by the **Kirchhoff relation** ($\alpha + \rho + \tau = 1$):
   - **Absorption (α):** Major fraction (~ 85–95%) enters the food and is converted to heat.
   - **Reflection (ρ):** A small fraction bounces back, particularly at glossy/oily surfaces.
   - **Transmission (τ):** A minor fraction passes through (significant only for thin/translucent foods).
4. **Internal Energy Deposition:** The absorbed IR excites vibrational modes of **O–H, C–H, and N–H bonds** in water, carbohydrates, proteins, and lipids, converting radiant energy directly into molecular kinetic energy (= heat).
5. **Heat Conduction:** Heat then conducts inward from the hot surface to the cooler bulk by Fourier conduction, raising the product's internal temperature.
6. **Moisture Removal:** If the surface temperature exceeds the boiling point of water, **evaporative cooling** occurs, accelerating the drying process.

**2. Role of Water as the Principal Absorber (3 marks):**

- Water has **strong absorption bands** at:
  - **2.9 – 3.1 µm** (O–H stretching vibration)
  - **6.0 – 6.1 µm** (H–O–H bending)
  - **> 12 µm** (librational modes)
- Foods with **high moisture content (> 70%)** absorb IR very efficiently ($\alpha_{water} \approx 0.95$).
- As moisture evaporates, the surface becomes **drier and more reflective** → the absorption efficiency drops, causing the heating rate to slow down — a phenomenon called **"case-hardening"** in IR drying.
- The **strong water absorption at 3 µm** is why mid-IR emitters operating near 1000 K are most efficient for drying wet foods.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A food product is to be heated using an IR emitter of surface area 0.05 m² and emissivity 0.95. The emitter is maintained at 1500 K, and the food surface is at 350 K with emissivity 0.90. Calculate the net radiative heat transfer rate. Take $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K}^4)$ and assume the surfaces are large and parallel.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $A = 0.05 \;\text{m}^2$
- $\varepsilon_s = 0.95$, $T_s = 1500 \;\text{K}$
- $\varepsilon_f = 0.90$, $T_f = 350 \;\text{K}$
- $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \;\text{W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K}^4)$

**Step 1 — Compute the 4th-power temperatures:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_s^{4} &= (1500)^{4} = 5.0625 \times 10^{12} \;\text{K}^4 \\
T_f^{4} &= (350)^{4} = 1.5006 \times 10^{10} \;\text{K}^4
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Net Heat Flux Using the Two-Surface Radiation Network Formula:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{net} &= A \cdot \frac{\sigma \left( T_s^{4} - T_f^{4} \right)}{\dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_s} + \dfrac{1}{\varepsilon_f} - 1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Denominator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{\varepsilon_s} + \frac{1}{\varepsilon_f} - 1 &= \frac{1}{0.95} + \frac{1}{0.90} - 1 \\
&= 1.0526 + 1.1111 - 1 \\
&= 1.1637
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Numerator:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sigma \left( T_s^{4} - T_f^{4} \right) &= 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times \left( 5.0625 \times 10^{12} - 1.5006 \times 10^{10} \right) \\
&= 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 5.0475 \times 10^{12} \\
&= 2.862 \times 10^{5} \;\text{W/m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the Net Heat Transfer Rate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
q_{net} &= 0.05 \times \frac{2.862 \times 10^{5}}{1.1637} \\
&= 0.05 \times 2.459 \times 10^{5} \\
&\approx 1.23 \times 10^{4} \;\text{W} \\
&\approx 12.3 \;\text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** The net radiative heat transfer rate is **≈ 12.3 kW**.

**Conclusion:** This high heat flux (246 kW/m²) confirms that IR heating delivers energy densities **orders of magnitude greater** than convective ovens (typically 5–20 kW/m²) — enabling very short processing times.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 (Alternative)]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With the help of a labelled diagram, explain the construction and working of an electric infrared emitter and a gas-fired infrared burner used in food processing.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Electric Infrared Emitter (3.5 marks):**

A typical **electric IR emitter** consists of the following components:

- **Heating Element:** A resistance-heated wire or coil made of **tungsten, nickel-chrome (NiCr), or iron-chrome-aluminum (FeCrAl)** wound into a helical or ribbon shape.
- **Refractory Substrate / Tube:** The element is embedded inside a **ceramic (alumina, silicon carbide) or quartz glass tube** that protects the element, provides mechanical rigidity, and acts as a secondary IR radiator.
- **Reflector:** A **polished aluminum or gold-plated parabolic reflector** behind the element directs > 80% of the emitted radiation toward the food, improving efficiency.
- **Terminal Housing:** End-caps with **electrical insulating ceramic bushings** and screw terminals for AC power connection.
- **Cooling Fins (optional):** Some high-power emitters include small cooling fins to prevent overheating of the housing.

**Working Principle:** When AC current passes through the resistance wire, **Joule heating** raises the element to **800 – 2500 K** (depending on design). At this temperature, it emits IR radiation with $\lambda_{\max}$ in the **0.8 – 3 µm range** (near- to mid-IR). The reflector focuses this radiation onto the food product, where it is absorbed and converted to heat.

**Advantages:** Rapid response (< 1 s), precise temperature control, no combustion byproducts, suitable for **clean-room food applications**.

**2. Gas-Fired Infrared Burner (3.5 marks):**

A **gas-fired IR burner** uses the **combustion of LPG or natural gas** as the heat source.

- **Construction:**
  - **Gas Inlet Manifold:** Distributes the gas uniformly across the burner face.
  - **Bunsen-Type Mixer / Venturi:** Mixes the gas with primary air in stoichiometric proportion.
  - **Porous Ceramic or Metal-Fibre Mat:** The premixed gas-air mixture passes through a **porous ceramic plate (e.g., cordierite)** or a **randomly packed bed of metal fibres** (typically FeCrAl). This acts as a **surface combustion medium** — the gas burns at the *surface* of the matrix, not as a free flame.
  - **Reflector:** A polished stainless-steel reflector above the burner directs IR downward onto the food.
  - **Ignition Electrode and Flame Sensor:** For safe auto-ignition and flame-failure detection.

- **Working Principle:** The gas-air mixture burns at the surface of the porous matrix, raising the matrix to **1100 – 1500 K**. Because the combustion occurs in a **millimeter-thick reaction zone at the matrix surface**, the **dominant heat-transfer mode is radiation** (not convection), giving a "radiant burner" effect. The matrix temperature determines $\lambda_{\max}$ via Wien's law, and the IR emitted is predominantly in the **near-IR (1 – 2 µm)**, providing **deeper penetration** into the food.

**Advantages:** Very high power density (up to 200 kW/m²), no electrical infrastructure needed, suitable for **continuous conveyorized drying tunnels**.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Discuss the advantages, limitations, and major food-processing applications of infrared heating.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Advantages of IR Heating (3 marks):**

- **High heat-transfer rate:** Energy flux at the food surface can reach **50 – 250 kW/m²** — far higher than conventional hot-air ovens (~ 5–20 kW/m²), reducing processing time by 30–90%.
- **Energy efficiency:** No need to preheat large volumes of air; 30–70% energy savings reported in drying and baking applications.
- **Rapid response:** Electric IR emitters reach operating temperature in **< 1 second**, enabling precise ON/OFF modulation and tight process control.
- **Uniform heating (with proper design):** Uniform emitter-to-product geometry and well-designed reflectors give highly uniform surface heating.
- **Compact equipment:** Small footprint compared to equivalent-throughput hot-air tunnels.
- **Preservation of nutrients:** Short exposure times retain heat-labile vitamins (e.g., **Vitamin C retention is 10–25% higher** in IR-dried fruits than in convective drying).
- **Environmentally clean:** No combustion byproducts at the food surface (with electric IR); no chlorofluorocarbons or solvents used.

**2. Limitations of IR Heating (2 marks):**

- **Limited penetration depth:** Only 0.1 – 10 mm — so IR alone is unsuitable for thick foods; must be combined with **conduction, microwave, or hot air** for bulk heating.
- **Surface overheating risk:** Excessive intensity causes **case-hardening, burning, or non-enzymatic browning** at the surface while the interior remains undercooked.
- **Uneven heating in 3D foods:** Corners and edges receive more radiation than central regions — requires **product rotation or scanning IR beams**.
- **Emitter degradation:** Tungsten and ceramic emitters degrade over time (~ 5000–10000 hours), reducing $\varepsilon$ and requiring periodic replacement.
- **Initial capital cost:** Higher than conventional gas ovens, though lifecycle cost is often lower.

**3. Major Food-Processing Applications (2 marks):**

| Application | Food Examples | Purpose |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Drying / Dehydration** | Fruits (banana, mango, apple), vegetables (onion, garlic), herbs, spices | Moisture removal; 50–80% faster than hot-air drying |
| **Baking** | Bread, biscuits, crackers, pizza | Crust browning, Maillard reaction, leavening |
| **Roasting / Toasting** | Nuts, coffee beans, sesame | Uniform browning, flavour development |
| **Blanching** | Vegetables (potato, carrot) | Enzyme inactivation with minimal nutrient loss |
| **Pasteurization / Sterilization** | Spices, flour, food-contact surfaces | Surface microbial reduction |
| **Tempering / Thawing** | Frozen meat, fish, fruit | Rapid, drip-loss reduction |
| **Disinfestation** | Grains, pulses | Surface disinfestation of insects |
| **Peeling** | Tomatoes, peaches, peppers | Thermal loosening of skin for mechanical peeling |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the combined-emissivity denominator** in the net heat-flux equation — applying only $\sigma (T_s^4 - T_f^4)$ without the $\left[ 1/\varepsilon_s + 1/\varepsilon_f - 1 \right]$ term results in **~ 2 to 3 mark deduction** per numerical.
> 2. **Confusing Stefan-Boltzmann constant value:** $5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ — students often write $5.67 \times 10^{-3}$ or omit the exponent entirely.
> 3. **Mixing units:** $T$ must be in **Kelvin (K)** for Stefan-Boltzmann; a common error is using °C and getting an order-of-magnitude wrong answer.
> 4. **Misclassifying IR regions:** The boundaries (0.7–1.4, 1.4–3, 3–8, 8–1000 µm) are frequently interchanged in answer sheets — be precise.
> 5. **Omitting the role of water absorption bands** (2.9 µm, 6.0 µm) when explaining the mechanism — examiners expect explicit mention of at least one specific water absorption wavelength for full credit.
> 6. **In numericals, forgetting to multiply by area $A$** to get $q_{net}$ in Watts (W) rather than $q$ in W/m².
> 7. **Diagram missing direction of energy flow or labels** — block diagrams without arrows or labels typically lose 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield KTU Revision Points — IR Heating**

- ✅ **Definition:** IR heating = thermal food processing using **electromagnetic radiation (0.7 – 1000 µm)** that is **absorbed at the food surface** and converted to heat.
- ✅ **No medium heating required** — air remains cool while the food heats; this is the key advantage over convection.
- ✅ **Three fundamental laws:** Planck's, Wien's ($\lambda_{\max} T = 2898 \, \mu\text{m·K}$), and Stefan-Boltzmann ($E = \varepsilon \sigma T^{4}$).
- ✅ **Stefan-Boltzmann constant:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W/(m}^2 \cdot \text{K}^4)$ — **always use Kelvin**.
- ✅ **Net heat-flux formula (most-tested):** $q_{net} = A \cdot \sigma (T_s^4 - T_f^4) / [1/\varepsilon_s + 1/\varepsilon_f - 1]$.
- ✅ **Penetration depth:** $\delta = 1/\mu$ — typically **0.1 – 10 mm** in foods; decreases with increasing $\lambda$ and moisture content.
- ✅ **Beer-Lambert law:** $I(x) = I_0 e^{-\mu x}$ — internal intensity decays exponentially.
- ✅ **Kirchhoff's law:** $\alpha = \varepsilon$ at thermal equilibrium — good absorbers are good emitters.
- ✅ **Wien's law application:** Hotter emitter → shorter $\lambda_{\max}$ → deeper penetration; cooler emitter → longer $\lambda_{\max}$ → shallower (surface) heating.
- ✅ **Water absorption bands:** **2.9 µm** (O–H stretch), **6.0 µm** (H–O–H bend) — match emitter to these for max efficiency.
- ✅ **IR emitter types:** Electric (tungsten, ceramic, quartz), gas-fired (porous ceramic matrix burner), catalytic (flameless, low-temperature).
- ✅ **Applications:** Drying, baking, roasting, blanching, pasteurization, tempering, peeling, disinfestation.
- ✅ **Penetration comparison:** Near-IR (1 µm) > Mid-IR (3 µm) > Far-IR (8 µm). **NIR penetrates 1000× deeper** than MIR in moist foods.
- ✅ **Energy balance for IR heating:** $m c_p dT/dt = q_{abs} - q_{loss} - q_{evap}$ — always include the **evaporative loss** term in drying problems.
- ✅ **Advantages to remember for 2-mark questions:** high heat flux, rapid response, energy efficiency, nutrient retention, compact equipment.
- ✅ **Limitations to remember for 2-mark questions:** shallow penetration, surface overheating (case-hardening), uneven 3D heating, emitter degradation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Dielectric Heating and Food Irradiation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT525 - NOVEL FOOD PROCESSING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Dielectric Heating and Food Irradiation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Dielectric Heating — Formal Definition

**Dielectric Heating** is an *electro-thermal* non-conventional food processing technique in which heat is generated **internally within a food material** (a dielectric/poor electrical conductor) when it is placed in a high-frequency alternating electromagnetic field. The heating occurs due to two coupled molecular phenomena — **dipole rotation** (alignment–relaxation of polar molecules, predominantly water) and **ionic conduction** (oscillatory drift of dissolved ions) — which together convert a portion of the absorbed electromagnetic energy into volumetric (in-situ) thermal energy.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Interpretation):**
> *Dielectric heating refers to the generation of heat within a non-conductive medium by the application of a high-frequency electric (oscillating) field. It includes two principal subclasses:*
> *(i) **Radio-Frequency (RF) Heating** at 1–100 MHz, and*
> *(ii) **Microwave (MW) Heating** at 300 MHz – 300 GHz.*

The two KTU-recognized industrial frequencies (ISM bands, reserved for Industrial, Scientific, Medical use to prevent radio interference) are:
- **915 MHz** (± 25 MHz band) — preferred for industrial-scale food processing due to deeper penetration.
- **2450 MHz** (± 50 MHz band) — used in domestic microwave ovens and laboratory systems.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> **The "Microwave Spinning Tops" Analogy 🎯**
>
> Imagine millions of tiny, water-filled spinning tops (water molecules are polar — they have a positive and a negative end, like a tiny magnet) inside your food. When you place the food in a high-frequency alternating electric field, the field's polarity flips **billions of times per second**. Each flip yanks the spinning top, trying to align it with the new field. Because the top cannot keep up perfectly, it tumbles and rubs against its neighbors — this internal friction is what we measure as **heat**. Result: the food cooks *from the inside out*, unlike a frying pan that cooks *from the outside in*.
>
> In the ionic conduction mechanism, dissolved salts (e.g., Na⁺, Cl⁻) act like "mini-rugby players" being pushed back and forth in the field, colliding with water molecules and generating heat through viscous drag.

### 1.3 Food Irradiation — Formal Definition

**Food Irradiation** is a *non-thermal preservation* technology defined by the Codex Alimentarius Commission (CAC) and adopted by KTU 2024 syllabus as: *the process of exposing prepackaged or bulk food to a controlled dose of ionizing radiation for the purpose of reducing microbial load, inhibiting sprouting, delaying ripening, controlling insect infestation, or achieving commercial sterility*. 

> [!NOTE]
> The internationally accepted unit of absorbed dose is the **Gray (Gy) = 1 J/kg of food material**. The older unit "rad" is deprecated: **1 Gy = 100 rad**.

### 1.4 Sources of Ionizing Radiation Permitted for Food

| Source | Type | Energy | Penetration | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Cobalt-60 (⁶⁰Co)** | γ-ray emitter | 1.17 MeV + 1.33 MeV | High | Most widely used commercially |
| **Caesium-137 (¹³⁷Cs)** | γ-ray emitter | 0.662 MeV | High | Byproduct of nuclear reactors |
| **Electron Beam (E-beam)** | β-rays | ≤ 10 MeV (FDA limit) | Low–Medium | High dose rate, no radioactive source |
| **X-rays** | Bremsstrahlung | ≤ 5 MeV (FDA limit) | High | Generated by striking e-beam on metal target |

> [!WARNING]
> KTU 2024 stress: **No radioactive material may directly contact the food.** All food irradiation is performed in shielded chambers with the source physically separated from the product.

### 1.5 Intuitive Analogy for Food Irradiation

> **The "Silent Bullet" Analogy 🎯**
>
> Think of ionizing radiation as a **silent, invisible bullet** that pierces through the food (just like an X-ray at a hospital) but, instead of producing an image, it damages the **DNA of microorganisms and insects**. The damaged DNA cannot replicate, so bacteria stop multiplying, parasites die, and weevil eggs never hatch. Crucially, the food itself, which is dead/non-living tissue, is essentially unharmed at the doses used. A simple rule of thumb: the *higher the dose*, the *more severe* the effect (sprout inhibition → pasteurization → sterilization).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanism of Dielectric Heating — Stepwise Logic

**Step 1 — Application of High-Frequency Field**
The food (treated as a lossy dielectric) is placed between two electrodes (for RF) or inside a waveguide/cavity (for MW). An alternating electric field $E$ at frequency $f$ is applied.

**Step 2 — Dipole Polarization (Dominant in MW)**
Polar molecules (chiefly $\text{H}_2\text{O}$) experience a torque $\tau = p \times E \sin\theta$, where $p$ is the permanent dipole moment of water ($p \approx 6.17 \times 10^{-30}\ \text{C·m}$). They attempt to align with the field. The relaxation time of water at 25 °C is $\tau_d \approx 9.2 \times 10^{-12}$ s.

**Step 3 — Dielectric Loss Factor (ε″)**
The complex relative permittivity is:
$$\varepsilon^* = \varepsilon' - j\,\varepsilon''$$

- $\varepsilon'$ = dielectric constant (energy storage capability)
- $\varepsilon''$ = dielectric loss factor (energy dissipation capability)

For a polar molecule, using Debye's model:
$$\varepsilon'' = (\varepsilon_s - \varepsilon_\infty)\,\frac{\omega \tau_d}{1 + (\omega \tau_d)^2}$$

where $\omega = 2\pi f$ is the angular frequency, $\varepsilon_s$ = static permittivity, $\varepsilon_\infty$ = infinite-frequency permittivity.

**Step 4 — Maximum Loss Condition**
The loss factor $\varepsilon''$ is maximum when $\omega \tau_d = 1$, i.e., the applied frequency matches the natural relaxation frequency of the molecule. This is the **resonance condition for optimal heating**.

**Step 5 — Power Dissipated per Unit Volume**
$$P = 2\pi f \cdot \varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon'' \cdot E^2 \cdot V \quad \text{[W]}$$

This is the **core KTU equation** for volumetric heat generation. Note the proportionality to $f$, $\varepsilon''$, and $E^2$.

**Step 6 — Penetration Depth**
Electromagnetic waves attenuate exponentially inside the food. The **penetration depth** $d_p$ is defined as the depth at which the power drops to $\frac{1}{e}$ (≈ 36.8%) of the surface value:
$$d_p = \frac{c}{2\pi f \sqrt{2\varepsilon'}}\,\sqrt{\sqrt{1 + \left(\frac{\varepsilon''}{\varepsilon'}\right)^2} - 1}$$

where $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s. At **2450 MHz**, $d_p \approx 1\text{–}3$ cm in high-moisture foods; at **915 MHz**, $d_p \approx 3\text{–}8$ cm.

### 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Dielectric Heating)

| # | Formula | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = 2\pi f \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon'' E^2 V$ | Volumetric power dissipation | W |
| 2 | $\tan\delta = \dfrac{\varepsilon''}{\varepsilon'}$ | Loss tangent (dissipation factor) | dimensionless |
| 3 | $d_p = \dfrac{c}{2\pi f \sqrt{2\varepsilon'}}\,\sqrt{\sqrt{1+\tan^2\delta} - 1}$ | Penetration depth (1/e) | m |
| 4 | $\varepsilon'' = \dfrac{(\varepsilon_s - \varepsilon_\infty)\,\omega\tau_d}{1+(\omega\tau_d)^2}$ | Debye loss factor | dimensionless |
| 5 | $P_{\max}$ when $\omega \tau_d = 1$ | Maximum power transfer condition | — |
| 6 | $Q = mc_p \Delta T = P \cdot t$ | Energy balance (heat = power × time) | J |

### 2.3 Mechanism of Food Irradiation — Stepwise Logic

**Step 1 — Generation of Ionizing Radiation**
- ⁶⁰Co decays via β⁻ emission to ⁶⁰Ni, releasing two γ-photons (1.17 and 1.33 MeV) per disintegration.
- Half-life of ⁶⁰Co: **T₁/₂ = 5.27 years** (1925 days).
- Activity: $A = A_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$, decay constant $\lambda = \frac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}}$.

**Step 2 — Interaction with Matter**
Ionizing radiation interacts with food via three mechanisms:
1. **Direct effect** — radiation directly hits and breaks microbial DNA.
2. **Indirect effect** — radiation ionizes water (the dominant food component): $\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \cdot\text{OH} + \text{H}^\cdot + e^-_{aq}$. The free radicals (·OH) then attack DNA, enzymes, and cell membranes.
3. **Secondary radiolysis products** — small amounts of $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$ formed contribute to microbial kill.

**Step 3 — Dose Classification (Codex/IAEA Standards)**

| Dose Range | Effect on Food | Example Applications |
|---|---|---|
| **Low dose (≤ 1 kGy)** | Sprout inhibition, insect disinfestation, delay ripening | Onions, potatoes, wheat, mangoes |
| **Medium dose (1 – 10 kGy)** | Pathogen reduction, shelf-life extension, parasite control | Meat, poultry, seafood, spices |
| **High dose (> 10 kGy)** | Commercial sterilization | Sterile hospital diets, spices for immuno-compromised patients |

> [!NOTE]
> The **D₁₀ value** is the radiation dose (in kGy) required to reduce the microbial population by 90% (i.e., one log cycle). For example, D₁₀ of *Salmonella* in ground beef is ≈ 0.5 – 0.7 kGy.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Food Irradiation)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $D = \dfrac{E_{\text{abs}}}{m}$ | Absorbed dose (definition) | Gy (J/kg) |
| 2 | $A = A_0 \cdot e^{-\lambda t}$ | Radioactive decay of source | Bq or Ci |
| 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{\ln 2}{T_{1/2}}$ | Decay constant | s⁻¹ |
| 4 | $A(t) = \dfrac{6.022 \times 10^{23} \cdot m_{\text{source}}}{M \cdot T_{1/2}}$ | Source activity from mass | Bq |
| 5 | $N = N_0 \cdot 10^{-D/D_{10}}$ | Survivor number after dose D | CFU/g |
| 6 | $D_{\text{req}} = D_{10} \cdot \log\left(\dfrac{N_0}{N}\right)$ | Required dose for log reduction | kGy |
| 7 | $S = A \cdot \overline{E}_\gamma \cdot \eta$ | Output of ⁶⁰Co source | W or Ci |
| 8 | $\eta$ (G-value) | Molecules formed per joule absorbed | mol/J |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Dielectric Heating** is industrially used for *thawing frozen blocks of meat, fish, and butter* (replacing time-consuming ambient thawing that risks microbial growth), *tempering frozen confections*, *baking biscuits and crackers*, *drying pasta*, *pasteurization of ready-to-eat meals*, and *disinfestation of grain*.
- **Food Irradiation** is approved in **> 60 countries** (including India, USA, China, EU) for spices, onions, potatoes, mangoes, poultry, and shelf-stable ready-to-eat products. India has operational plants at Vashi (Navi Mumbai) and Lasalgaon, Nashik.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Power Dissipated in a Dielectric Material

We derive the **volumetric power dissipation equation** in a lossy dielectric subjected to a sinusoidal electric field $\vec{E}(t) = E_0 \cos(\omega t)$.

**Step 1 — Energy Density in the Dielectric**
The instantaneous energy density stored in a dielectric due to polarization is:
$$u(t) = \frac{1}{2}\,\varepsilon_0\,\varepsilon'\,E^2(t)$$

**Step 2 — Complex Representation of the Field**
Convert the real field to its complex phasor form:
$$\tilde{E}(t) = E_0\,e^{j\omega t}$$

**Step 3 — Current Density in a Lossy Dielectric**
The total current density $\vec{J}$ has two components:
$$\vec{J} = \vec{J}_{\text{disp}} + \vec{J}_{\text{cond}} = j\omega\varepsilon_0\varepsilon'\,\vec{E} + \omega\varepsilon_0\varepsilon''\,\vec{E}$$

The first term is the reactive (capacitive) part, the second is the **lossy (conductive) part** that dissipates real power.

**Step 4 — Time-Average Power Density**
The complex Poynting theorem gives the average power dissipated per unit volume:
$$p_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{2}\,\omega\,\varepsilon_0\,\varepsilon''\,E_0^2$$

**Step 5 — Total Power for Volume V**
Multiplying by the volume of the dielectric load:
$$\boxed{P = \omega\,\varepsilon_0\,\varepsilon''\,E_0^{\,2}\,V = 2\pi f\,\varepsilon_0\,\varepsilon''\,E^2\,V}$$

Substituting $\omega = 2\pi f$, this is the canonical KTU result. Every parameter in this equation is a KTU 2024 high-yield item.

### 3.2 Derivation — Penetration Depth of an EM Wave in Food

Consider a plane electromagnetic wave propagating in the +z direction through a lossy dielectric of complex permittivity $\varepsilon^* = \varepsilon' - j\varepsilon''$. The wave has the form:
$$E(z) = E_0\,e^{-\alpha z}\,e^{-j\beta z}$$

**Step 1 — Wave Equation in the Medium**
The complex propagation constant is:
$$\gamma = j\omega\sqrt{\mu_0\varepsilon_0\varepsilon^*} = \alpha + j\beta$$

**Step 2 — Solving for α and β**
For a non-magnetic food ($\mu = \mu_0$):
$$\alpha = \omega\sqrt{\frac{\mu_0\varepsilon_0\varepsilon'}{2}}\,\sqrt{\sqrt{1 + \tan^2\delta} - 1}$$
$$\beta = \omega\sqrt{\frac{\mu_0\varepsilon_0\varepsilon'}{2}}\,\sqrt{\sqrt{1 + \tan^2\delta} + 1}$$

**Step 3 — Power Decay and Definition of d_p**
Power density is proportional to $|E|^2$, hence:
$$P(z) = P_0\,e^{-2\alpha z}$$

Penetration depth is the depth at which power drops to $1/e$:
$$e^{-2\alpha d_p} = e^{-1} \;\Longrightarrow\; d_p = \frac{1}{2\alpha}$$

**Step 4 — Final Form of d_p**
Using $\mu_0\varepsilon_0 = 1/c^2$ and $f = \omega / 2\pi$:
$$\boxed{d_p = \frac{c}{2\pi f\,\sqrt{2\varepsilon'}}\,\sqrt{\sqrt{1 + \tan^2\delta} - 1}}$$

This is the second KTU 2024 canonical result.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example 1 — Penetration Depth in Beef

**Given:** Beef at 25 °C has $\varepsilon' = 52$, $\varepsilon'' = 17$ at 2450 MHz. Compute the penetration depth.

**Solution:**
$$\tan\delta = \frac{17}{52} = 0.3269$$
$$\tan^2\delta = 0.1069$$
$$\sqrt{1 + 0.1069} = \sqrt{1.1069} = 1.0521$$
$$1.0521 - 1 = 0.0521$$
$$\sqrt{0.0521} = 0.2283$$
$$\sqrt{2 \times 52} = \sqrt{104} = 10.198$$
$$d_p = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{2\pi \times 2.45 \times 10^9 \times 10.198 \times 0.2283}$$

Compute the denominator stepwise:
$$2\pi \times 2.45 \times 10^9 = 1.539 \times 10^{10}$$
$$1.539 \times 10^{10} \times 10.198 = 1.5695 \times 10^{11}$$
$$1.5695 \times 10^{11} \times 0.2283 = 3.583 \times 10^{10}$$
$$d_p = \frac{3 \times 10^8}{3.583 \times 10^{10}} = 8.37 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{m} \approx 8.4\ \text{mm}$$

**Result:** $d_p \approx 8.4$ mm in beef at 2450 MHz, confirming the textbook value of ≈ 8 mm.

### 3.4 Derivation — Source Activity Required for a Given Dose Rate

To design a ⁶⁰Co food irradiator delivering a target dose rate $\dot{D}$ (Gy/s) to a food mass $m$ at a distance $r$ from a point source, the required activity is derived as follows.

**Step 1 — Dose Rate from a Point Source**
$$\dot{D} = \frac{A \cdot \Gamma}{r^2}$$

where $\Gamma$ is the specific gamma-ray dose constant of ⁶⁰Co, $\Gamma = 2.56 \times 10^{-18}$ Gy·m²/(Bq·s).

**Step 2 — Required Activity**
$$\boxed{A = \frac{\dot{D}\,r^2}{\Gamma}}$$

**Step 3 — Mass Equivalent of Cobalt-60**
If $A$ is in Bq and $T_{1/2}$ is in seconds, the required mass of ⁶⁰Co metal is:
$$m_{\text{Co}} = \frac{A \cdot M_{\text{Co}} \cdot T_{1/2}}{6.022 \times 10^{23} \cdot \ln 2}$$

with $M_{\text{Co}} = 59.93$ g/mol.

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example 2 — Cobalt-60 Source Sizing

**Given:** An irradiator must deliver a minimum dose of $D = 1$ kGy to a $5$ kg food packet in $t = 1$ hour at a distance of $r = 0.5$ m from a ⁶⁰Co source. Calculate the required activity.

**Solution:**
$$\dot{D} = \frac{D}{t} = \frac{1000\ \text{Gy}}{3600\ \text{s}} = 0.2778\ \text{Gy/s}$$
$$A = \frac{\dot{D} \cdot r^2}{\Gamma} = \frac{0.2778 \times 0.25}{2.56 \times 10^{-18}} = 2.71 \times 10^{16}\ \text{Bq}$$

Convert to Curie: $1\ \text{Ci} = 3.7 \times 10^{10}$ Bq
$$A = \frac{2.71 \times 10^{16}}{3.7 \times 10^{10}} = 7.32 \times 10^{5}\ \text{Ci} \approx 732\ \text{kCi}$$

**Equivalent mass of ⁶⁰Co:**
$$m_{\text{Co}} = \frac{2.71 \times 10^{16} \times 59.93 \times 5.27 \times 365.25 \times 86400}{6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 0.693}$$

Compute stepwise:
$$5.27 \times 365.25 \times 86400 = 1.663 \times 10^{8}\ \text{s}$$
$$2.71 \times 10^{16} \times 59.93 = 1.624 \times 10^{18}$$
$$1.624 \times 10^{18} \times 1.663 \times 10^{8} = 2.701 \times 10^{26}$$
$$6.022 \times 10^{23} \times 0.693 = 4.173 \times 10^{23}$$
$$m_{\text{Co}} = \frac{2.701 \times 10^{26}}{4.173 \times 10^{23}} \approx 647\ \text{g}$$

**Result:** Approximately **647 g of ⁶⁰Co** is needed to deliver 1 kGy to 5 kg of food in 1 hour at 0.5 m.

### 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Microwave (MW) | Radio-Frequency (RF) | E-beam Irradiation | Gamma Irradiation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency / Energy | 915 / 2450 MHz | 13.56 / 27.12 MHz | 5–10 MeV | 1.17 + 1.33 MeV |
| Heating/Cooling | Heating (thermal) | Heating (thermal) | Non-thermal | Non-thermal |
| Penetration | Shallow (cm) | Deeper (m) | Low (cm) | High (tens of cm) |
| Mechanism | Dipole + ionic | Dipole + ionic | Direct DNA damage | Indirect (radiolysis) |
| Process Type | Preservation | Pasteurization | Sterilization | Sterilization/Pasteurization |
| Industrial Use | Ovens, drying | Thawing, baking | Spices, meats | Spices, fruits |
| Capital Cost | Low | Low | Very High | Very High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEFTT525]**
**Define dielectric heating. Differentiate between RF heating and microwave heating.**

**Model Answer:**
Dielectric heating is the generation of heat within a non-conductive (dielectric) food material when placed in a high-frequency alternating electric field, caused by dipole rotation and ionic conduction. The two KTU-recognized variants are:

| Feature | RF Heating | Microwave Heating |
|---|---|---|
| Frequency | 1 – 100 MHz (industrial: 27.12 MHz) | 300 MHz – 300 GHz (industrial: 915 / 2450 MHz) |
| Penetration depth | Larger (m-scale) | Smaller (cm-scale) |
| Electrode type | Plate electrodes | Waveguide/cavity |
| Industrial use | Thawing, baking, drying | Domestic ovens, drying pasta |

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEFTT525]**
**What is the D₁₀ value in food irradiation? List the three permitted sources of ionizing radiation for food.**

**Model Answer:**
The D₁₀ value is the radiation dose (in kGy) required to reduce the microbial population by 90% (1 log cycle). It is a measure of radiation sensitivity of a microorganism.
The three internationally permitted sources of ionizing radiation for food are:
1. **Gamma rays** from Cobalt-60 (1.17 + 1.33 MeV) and Caesium-137 (0.662 MeV)
2. **Electron beams** (β-rays) of energy up to 10 MeV
3. **X-rays** (bremsstrahlung) of energy up to 5 MeV

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, PEFTT525, Module 3]**
> *Answer the following. Each sub-question carries 7 marks.*

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1)**

**(a)** Explain the principle and mechanism of dielectric heating. Derive the equation for power dissipated per unit volume in a dielectric material. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** Dielectric heating is a volumetric (internal) heating method based on the interaction of a high-frequency alternating electric field (RF or microwave) with a food material treated as a lossy dielectric.

**Mechanism (two coupled phenomena):**
1. **Dipole rotation** — Polar molecules (chiefly water) try to follow the alternating field; the lag between alignment and field reversal produces frictional heat.
2. **Ionic conduction** — Dissolved ions oscillate under the field, colliding with neighbouring molecules and dissipating kinetic energy as heat.

**Derivation of Power Equation:**

The complex permittivity is $\varepsilon^* = \varepsilon' - j\varepsilon''$. The applied field $\tilde{E}(t) = E_0 e^{j\omega t}$ produces a total current density:
$$J = j\omega\varepsilon_0\varepsilon'\,\vec{E} + \omega\varepsilon_0\varepsilon''\,\vec{E}$$

The real (in-phase) component $\omega\varepsilon_0\varepsilon''$ represents conduction loss, while the imaginary $j\omega\varepsilon_0\varepsilon'$ represents reactive (capacitive) storage.

Applying the complex Poynting theorem, the time-averaged dissipated power per unit volume is:
$$p_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{2}\,\omega\,\varepsilon_0\,\varepsilon''\,E_0^2$$

For a load of volume $V$:
$$\boxed{P = 2\pi f\,\varepsilon_0\,\varepsilon''\,E^2\,V}$$

This is the KTU-canonical power equation. Note that $P \propto f \cdot \varepsilon'' \cdot E^2$.

**(b)** A 0.5 kg food sample with $\varepsilon' = 50$, $\varepsilon'' = 15$, and density 1000 kg/m³ is heated in a microwave oven at 2450 MHz with an electric field of $E = 5000$ V/m. Calculate the power dissipated and the time required to raise the temperature by 30 °C. Take $c_p = 3500$ J/(kg·K), $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Volume of sample:**
$$V = \frac{m}{\rho} = \frac{0.5}{1000} = 5 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Power dissipated:**
$$P = 2\pi f\,\varepsilon_0\,\varepsilon''\,E^2\,V$$
$$P = 2\pi \times 2.45 \times 10^9 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \times 15 \times (5000)^2 \times 5 \times 10^{-4}$$

Compute stepwise:
$$2\pi \times 2.45 \times 10^9 = 1.539 \times 10^{10}$$
$$1.539 \times 10^{10} \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} = 0.1363$$
$$0.1363 \times 15 = 2.0445$$
$$5000^2 = 2.5 \times 10^7$$
$$2.0445 \times 2.5 \times 10^7 = 5.111 \times 10^7$$
$$5.111 \times 10^7 \times 5 \times 10^{-4} = 2.556 \times 10^4\ \text{W}$$

**Result:** $P \approx 25.56$ kW

**Step 3 — Energy required for ΔT = 30 °C:**
$$Q = m\,c_p\,\Delta T = 0.5 \times 3500 \times 30 = 52{,}500\ \text{J} = 52.5\ \text{kJ}$$

**Step 4 — Time required:**
$$t = \frac{Q}{P} = \frac{52{,}500}{25{,}560} \approx 2.05\ \text{s}$$

**Result:** $t \approx 2.05$ seconds to heat the sample by 30 °C.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2)**

**(a)** Discuss the principle of food irradiation. Explain the direct and indirect effects of ionizing radiation on microbial cells. Classify the dose ranges with examples. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** Food irradiation is a *non-thermal* preservation technique in which food is exposed to controlled doses of ionizing radiation. The radiation damages the DNA of microorganisms, parasites, and insects, while leaving the food matrix (which is essentially non-living tissue) substantially intact.

**Effects on microbial cells:**

1. **Direct effect:** The ionizing radiation (γ-photon or β-particle) physically strikes the DNA molecule, breaking phosphodiester bonds and creating *single-strand breaks* and *double-strand breaks*. The cell becomes unable to replicate or transcribe genes, leading to inactivation. A typical bacterium requires 1–5 kGy for inactivation.

2. **Indirect effect:** The radiation ionizes intracellular water (the most abundant cellular component):
$$\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\gamma} \cdot\text{OH} + \text{H}^\cdot + e^-_{aq}$$
The hydroxyl radical (·OH) is highly reactive and attacks DNA bases, enzymes, lipid membranes, and proteins, causing oxidative damage. Secondary radiolysis products (e.g., $\text{H}_2\text{O}_2$) also contribute to microbial kill. In high-moisture foods, the indirect effect is the *dominant* killing mechanism.

**Dose Classification (Codex Alimentarius):**

| Range | Dose | Effect | Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low | ≤ 1 kGy | Sprout inhibition, insect disinfestation | Onion, potato, wheat |
| Medium | 1 – 10 kGy | Pathogen reduction, parasite control | Spices, chicken, fish |
| High | > 10 kGy | Commercial sterilization | Hospital sterile diets, spices for immunocompromised patients |

**(b)** The initial microbial load of a spice sample is $N_0 = 10^6$ CFU/g. The D₁₀ value of the contaminating *Salmonella* is 0.6 kGy. Calculate (i) the dose required for a 5-log reduction and (ii) the survivor count after 2.4 kGy. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Dose for 5-log reduction:**
$$D_{\text{req}} = D_{10} \times \log\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right)$$
$$D_{\text{req}} = 0.6 \times \log\left(\frac{10^6}{10^1}\right) = 0.6 \times 5 = 3.0\ \text{kGy}$$

**Result:** $D_{\text{req}} = 3.0$ kGy

**(ii) Survivor count after D = 2.4 kGy:**
$$N = N_0 \times 10^{-D/D_{10}}$$
$$N = 10^6 \times 10^{-2.4/0.6} = 10^6 \times 10^{-4} = 10^2\ \text{CFU/g}$$

**Result:** Survivors = 100 CFU/g (4-log reduction achieved).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing ε′ and ε″** — ε′ is the *storage* term; ε″ is the *loss* term. Mixing them up costs 2 marks in derivation questions.
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 1/2** in the time-averaged Poynting theorem derivation. Many students write $P = \omega\varepsilon_0\varepsilon''E^2V$ and lose 1 mark. The correct is $P = \frac{1}{2}\omega\varepsilon_0\varepsilon''E^2V = 2\pi f\varepsilon_0\varepsilon''E^2V$ (note that $E$ is peak; if $E$ is RMS, the factor is 1, not 1/2).
> 3. **Penetration depth formula** — Students often forget the $\sqrt{2\varepsilon'}$ in the denominator. This is a 3-mark error in numericals.
> 4. **D₁₀ in log reduction** — D₁₀ is for **one** log cycle; for an $n$-log reduction, multiply by $n$. Confusing it with "dose for complete kill" is a common KTU trap.
> 5. **Source activity vs dose rate** — Activity is in Bq/Ci, dose rate is in Gy/s. These are **not** the same; you must use the gamma constant Γ of ⁶⁰Co to convert.
> 6. **Decay of source** — Forgetting that ⁶⁰Co loses 1.2% activity per month (since $T_{1/2} = 5.27$ yr) means the plant must be re-loaded periodically.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Dielectric heating = volumetric heating** (unlike conductive/convective which are surface-based).
- ✅ Two **ISM frequencies**: **915 MHz** (industrial, deeper penetration) and **2450 MHz** (domestic, shallower penetration).
- ✅ **Two heating mechanisms**: dipole rotation (dominant) + ionic conduction.
- ✅ **Core equation**: $P = 2\pi f\,\varepsilon_0\,\varepsilon''\,E^2 V$. Power ∝ frequency, loss factor, and *square* of field.
- ✅ **Penetration depth** $d_p$ is the depth at which power drops to 1/e (≈ 37%); higher frequency = lower $d_p$.
- ✅ **Maximum heating** occurs at the **resonance condition** $\omega\tau_d = 1$.
- ✅ **Food irradiation** is a **non-thermal, cold process** — food does not become radioactive.
- ✅ **Three sources** of ionizing radiation: ⁶⁰Co, ¹³⁷Cs, e-beam, X-rays.
- ✅ **Unit of absorbed dose** is **Gray (Gy)** = 1 J/kg. 1 Gy = 100 rad.
- ✅ **Dose ranges**: Low (≤ 1 kGy), Medium (1–10 kGy), High (> 10 kGy).
- ✅ **D₁₀** = dose for 90% kill (1 log); D_required = D₁₀ × n (for n-log reduction).
- ✅ **⁶⁰Co half-life** = 5.27 years; emits **two** γ-photons (1.17 + 1.33 MeV) per decay.
- ✅ **Food irradiation** does NOT make food radioactive — energy is below nuclear activation threshold.
- ✅ **International Radura symbol** is mandatory on labels of irradiated food sold internationally.
- ✅ **Industrial applications**: dielectric — thawing, baking, drying; irradiation — sprout inhibition (onion, potato), pathogen control (spices, meat), commercial sterilization.
- ✅ **Direct effect** = DNA strand breaks; **Indirect effect** = water radiolysis → ·OH radicals → oxidative damage.
- ✅ **Advantages of irradiation**: cold process, no chemical residue, deep penetration, packaged food can be treated.
- ✅ **Disadvantages of irradiation**: consumer resistance, high capital cost, requires dosimetry, regulatory restrictions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effects on Foods and Applications of Irradiation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEFTT525 - NOVEL FOOD PROCESSING TECHNOLOGY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Effects on Foods and Applications of Irradiation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is Food Irradiation?

**Food Irradiation** is a non-thermal physical method of food preservation that involves exposing foodstuffs to controlled doses of ionizing radiation—primarily **gamma rays (from Cobalt-60 or Cesium-137)**, **accelerated electrons**, or **X-rays**. The primary goal is to inactivate spoilage microorganisms, pathogenic bacteria, parasites, and insects; inhibit sprouting and ripening; and extend the shelf life of food while preserving its nutritional and sensory quality.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Irradiation is officially classified by **Codex Alimentarius** and the **Atomic Energy Regulatory Board (AERB), India** as a food processing technology, not a food additive. It is governed under the **Atomic Energy (Radiation Processing of Food and Allied Products) Rules, 2012** in India.

**Standard Dose Metrics Used in Food Irradiation:**

| Metric | Symbol | Value |
|---|---|---|
| **Gray (Gy)** | Gy | **1 Gy = 1 J/kg** of absorbed energy |
| **Kilo Gray** | kGy | **1 kGy = 1000 Gy = 1000 J/kg** |
| **Mega Gray** | MGy | 1 MGy = 10⁶ Gy |

> [!NOTE]
> The legal upper dose limit prescribed by **WHO/FAO/IAEA Joint Expert Committee** for any food is **10 kGy**, which is considered safe and nutritionally acceptable.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Sun-Bathed Mango"

Imagine you leave a freshly cut **mango slice** on the kitchen counter for an hour. Natural sunlight (a weak, non-ionizing form of radiation) slightly dries the surface and slows microbial growth. **Food irradiation does a similar job but with extreme precision and speed**: the food is passed through a shielded chamber where precisely controlled **ionizing radiation** zaps microorganisms, insects, and enzymes at the molecular level—without significantly heating the food.

Think of it this way:
- **Microwave** = heats water molecules (thermal).
- **Irradiation** = breaks chemical bonds silently, like a *molecular sniper* (non-thermal).

The food emerges microbiologically safer, longer-lasting, and nearly identical in taste, color, and nutrition.

> [!TIP]
> The famous **"Radura" symbol** (the green flower-like logo) is internationally used on irradiated foods to inform consumers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanisms of Action of Ionizing Radiation

Ionizing radiation interacts with food in three fundamental ways:

1. **Direct Effect:** Radiation directly hits and ionizes a target molecule (e.g., DNA, enzyme, lipid), causing bond cleavage.
2. **Indirect Effect:** Radiation first ionizes **water** (the major food component, ~70–80%) producing reactive species, which then attack food molecules.
3. **Combined Effect:** Both direct and indirect actions occur simultaneously.

The **indirect effect dominates** in moist foods due to radiolysis of water:

$$H_2O \xrightarrow{\text{ionizing radiation}} H^\bullet + OH^\bullet + e^-_{aq} + H_2 + H_2O_2$$

These **reactive oxygen species (ROS)** like hydroxyl radical ($OH^\bullet$) and hydrogen peroxide ($H_2O_2$) attack:
- Microbial DNA → microbial death
- Unsaturated lipids → lipid peroxidation (off-flavor)
- Sulfhydryl (-SH) groups in proteins → texture changes

### 2.2 Classification of Radiation Dose Effects on Foods

| Dose Range (kGy) | Biological Effect | Example Application |
|---|---|---|
| **0.05 – 0.15** | Sprout inhibition | Potatoes, onions, garlic |
| **0.15 – 0.50** | Insect disinfestation | Cereals, pulses, dried fruits |
| **0.50 – 1.00** | Delay of ripening | Mangoes, bananas, papayas |
| **1.00 – 3.00** | Reduction of spoilage microbes | Fresh meat, poultry, fish |
| **1.00 – 5.00** | Reduction of pathogens | Frozen seafood, spices |
| **5.00 – 10.00** | Sterilization | Hospital diets, spices |
| **>10.00** | Industrial sterilization | Pet food, animal feed |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Absorbed Dose** | $D = \frac{E}{m}$ | D = Absorbed dose (Gy); E = Energy absorbed (J); m = Mass (kg) |
| **Microbial Log Reduction** | $\log(N) = \log(N_0) - kD$ | N = survivors; $N_0$ = initial count; k = dose constant; D = dose |
| **D-Value Concept** | $D_{10} = \frac{1}{k}$ | Dose needed to kill 90% (1-log) of a microbial population |
| **Decimal Reduction Dose** | $D = \frac{\log(N_0/N)}{k}$ | Similar to above, used in sterilization |
| **G-Value** | $G = \frac{\text{Number of molecules changed}}{\text{Energy absorbed (100 eV)}}$ | Radiation chemical yield |
| **LET (Linear Energy Transfer)** | $LET = \frac{\Delta E}{\Delta x}$ | Energy deposited per unit path length (keV/μm) |
| **Total Dose Received** | $D_{total} = \dot{D} \times t$ | $\dot{D}$ = dose rate (Gy/s); t = time (s) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **D₁₀ value** is a critical concept in KTU exams. For example, *Salmonella* has a $D_{10}$ of about **0.5–0.7 kGy**, meaning 0.5–0.7 kGy is needed to reduce its count by 90% (one log).

### 2.4 Effects on Major Food Constituents

**A. Effect on Water**
- Primary radiolysis produces free radicals.
- Major contributor to indirect effects.

**B. Effect on Carbohydrates**
- Starch undergoes depolymerization → reduced viscosity.
- Cellulose becomes more soluble.
- Simple sugars may show slight browning (Maillard-like).

**C. Effect on Proteins**
- Disruption of disulfide bonds (-S-S-) and hydrogen bonds.
- Possible denaturation and loss of solubility.
- Essential amino acids (lysine, methionine) may be marginally reduced at high doses.

**D. Effect on Lipids**
- **Most sensitive** food component.
- Unsaturated fatty acids undergo **peroxidation** producing off-odors and rancidity.
- Formation of carbonyls and hydroperoxides.

**E. Effect on Vitamins**
- Fat-soluble vitamins (A, E) more sensitive than water-soluble (C, B-complex).
- Vitamin losses comparable to conventional thermal processing (often <10–15%).

**F. Effect on Enzymes**
- Inactivated at high doses (>5 kGy).
- Cause of softening in some fruits due to pectinase activity.

### 2.5 Effect on Microorganisms

| Order of Sensitivity | Microorganisms | Relative Resistance |
|---|---|---|
| Most sensitive | Viruses, vegetative bacteria | Low |
| Moderate | Yeasts, molds, bacterial spores | Medium |
| Most resistant | *Deinococcus radiodurans*, certain enzymes | Very high |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** Food irradiation is used in commercial cold-chain logistics to enhance the safety of ready-to-eat meals, hospital diets for immunocompromised patients, and space food for astronauts (NASA-approved).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Microbial Log-Reduction Equation

The survival of microorganisms follows first-order kinetics with respect to radiation dose.

**Step 1: Start with the basic first-order differential equation:**

$$\frac{dN}{dD} = -k \cdot N$$

**Step 2: Separate variables to integrate:**

$$\frac{dN}{N} = -k \cdot dD$$

**Step 3: Integrate both sides from initial count $N_0$ to survivors N over dose range 0 to D:**

$$\int_{N_0}^{N} \frac{dN}{N} = -k \int_{0}^{D} dD$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the integrals (logarithmic on left, linear on right):**

$$\ln(N) - \ln(N_0) = -kD$$

**Step 5: Rearrange using properties of logarithms:**

$$\ln\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -kD$$

**Step 6: Convert to base-10 logarithm (since microbiologists use log₁₀):**

$$\log\left(\frac{N}{N_0}\right) = -\frac{kD}{2.303}$$

**Step 7: Express survivors in terms of initial count and dose:**

$$N = N_0 \cdot 10^{-kD/2.303}$$

**Step 8: Define the D₁₀ value (decimal reduction dose) as the dose for 1-log reduction:**

$$D_{10} = \frac{2.303}{k}$$

**Step 9: Substitute back to get the final classical form used in food irradiation:**

$$\log(N) = \log(N_0) - \frac{D}{D_{10}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This equation is **HIGH WEIGHTAGE in KTU exams** for numerical problems on radiation dose calculations.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A sample of fresh fish contains an initial bacterial load of $10^6$ CFU/g of *Salmonella*. The D₁₀ value for *Salmonella* in fish is **0.65 kGy**. Calculate:
- (i) The dose required to reduce the count to **100 CFU/g** (i.e., 4-log reduction).
- (ii) The dose required for a **5-log reduction** (to 10 CFU/g).

**Solution:**

**Part (i): 4-log reduction**

$$\log\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = \frac{D}{D_{10}}$$

$$\log\left(\frac{10^6}{10^2}\right) = \frac{D}{0.65}$$

$$\log(10^4) = \frac{D}{0.65}$$

$$4 = \frac{D}{0.65}$$

$$D = 4 \times 0.65 = 2.6 \text{ kGy}$$

**Part (ii): 5-log reduction**

$$\log\left(\frac{10^6}{10^1}\right) = \frac{D}{0.65}$$

$$5 = \frac{D}{0.65}$$

$$D = 5 \times 0.65 = 3.25 \text{ kGy}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** To make fish microbiologically safe at consumer level, an irradiation dose of **2.6–3.25 kGy** is sufficient, which is well within the **10 kGy** regulatory limit.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Effect vs. Food Constituent

| Food Constituent | Sensitivity | Major Effect at 1 kGy | Major Effect at 5 kGy |
|---|---|---|---|
| Water | High | Radiolysis to ROS | Same |
| Carbohydrates | Low-Moderate | Slight depolymerization | Browning possible |
| Proteins | Moderate | Minor denaturation | Loss of -SH groups |
| Lipids | **Highest** | Off-odor onset | Strong rancidity |
| Vitamins | Moderate | <5% loss (A, E) | <15% loss |
| Enzymes | Moderate-High | Partial inactivation | Mostly inactivated |
| DNA (Microbes) | Very High | Microbial death | Complete sterilization |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Mechanism of Food Irradiation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Food Sample] --> B[Shielded Irradiation Chamber]
    B --> C{Radiation Source}
    C --> C1[Co-60 Gamma Rays]
    C --> C2[Cs-137 Gamma Rays]
    C --> C3[Electron Beam 10 MeV]
    C --> C4[X-rays 5 MeV]
    B --> D[Energy Absorption in Food]
    D --> D1[Direct Ionization of Molecules]
    D --> D2[Radiolysis of Water]
    D1 --> E[DNA Damage in Microbes]
    D1 --> F[Lipid Peroxidation]
    D2 --> G[Reactive Oxygen Species ROS]
    G --> E
    G --> F
    E --> H[Microbial Inactivation]
    F --> I[Quality Changes]
    H --> J[Safe, Shelf-Stable Food]
    I --> J
    J --> K[Quality Check and Packaging]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Dose-Application Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Dose 0.05-0.15 kGy] --> B1[Sprout Inhibition]
    A2[Dose 0.15-0.5 kGy] --> B2[Insect Disinfestation]
    A3[Dose 0.5-1.0 kGy] --> B3[Ripening Delay]
    A4[Dose 1.0-3.0 kGy] --> B4[Spoilage Microbe Control]
    A5[Dose 1.0-5.0 kGy] --> B5[Pathogen Reduction]
    A6[Dose 5.0-10.0 kGy] --> B6[Commercial Sterilization]
    A7[Dose greater 10 kGy] --> B7[Industrial Sterilization]
```

### 4.3 Schematic — Process Flow of a Commercial Food Irradiation Plant

